body:
I see. Ya so HF (last time I checked) didn't easily support stop sequences. So one way around this is something like

```
response = manifest.run(prompt, **kwargs, return_response=True)
if stop:
    # Assuming the goal is to get the first stop token
    shortest_ans = sorted([response.get_response(st) for st in stop], key=len)
    ans = shortest_ans[0]
else:
    ans = response.get_response()
return ans
```

body:
okay im taking a look now. my biggest thought is around the interface for __call__ on the LLM class. right now, __call__ is really simplistic in that it just returns the simplest possible thing (the most likely string). however, this is not really the FULL output of the LLM - the full output would be the N most likely things, as well as other things that ive been meaning to add in like log probs

i would love to (1) define what a full, complete response would look like (N most likely things, logprobs, anything else?), (2) define a separate method on the LLM that returns this (the reason i dont want to change the current __call__ is for a combination of backwards compability + ease of use - i do think the single string return is useful in enough place where its handy to keep it around

body:
i would do something like
```
@classmethod
def from_model_id(model_id: str, task: Optional[str] = None, model_kwargs: Optional[dict] = None, **kwargs):
    # construct pipeline here
    return cls(pipeline=pipeline, **kwargs)
```

this allows users to do either

`HuggingFacePipeline(pipeline=pipeline)` or `HuggingFacePipeline.from_model_id("gpt2")`

i think if we do this, we can also just delete the above validate_environment call

i think we also no longer need to save the model_id, model_kwargs, and task param on the class (which simplifies the interface imensely)

body:
It does get passed in to the child `LLMChain` but not the `LLMChain`'s `LLM`:

```
    manager = CallbackManager([handler])
    tool = "Search"
    responses = [
        f"FooBarBaz\nAction: {tool}\nAction Input: misalignment",
        "Oh well\nAction: Final Answer\nAction Input: curses foiled again",
    ]
    fake_llm = FakeListLLM(responses=responses, callback_manager=manager, verbose=True)
    tools = [
        Tool("Search", lambda x: x, "Useful for searching"),
    ]
    agent = initialize_agent(
        tools,
        fake_llm,
        agent="zero-shot-react-description",
        verbose=True,
        callback_manager=manager,
    )
```

^ this is the pattern you'd have to follow to get the callback manager to all children modules of the `AgentExecutor` (pass in `CallbackManager` to `LLM`) essentially.

see here for more details: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/db5c8e0c42d9c38ea1373f1c0355698527350e61/langchain/agents/agent.py#L135

body:
Yes that pattern works fine when initialising, it's just annoying when you have to change the callback manager across the board for an existing agent that you don't want to throw away (because eg you want to keep memory)

ie. something like this

```py
class MyLangchainAgent:
    agent_executor: AgentExecutor
    other_langchain_objects: list[Chain | BaseLLM] = []

    def __init__(self, state: dict, loop: asyncio.AbstractEventLoop):
        super().__init__(state, loop)

        llm = OpenAI(temperature=0, verbose=True)
        llm_math_chain = LLMMathChain(llm=llm, verbose=True)
        tools = [
            Tool(
                name="Calculator",
                func=llm_math_chain.run,
                description="useful for when you need to answer questions about math",
            ),
        ]
        self.agent_executor = initialize_agent(
            tools, llm, agent="zero-shot-react-description", verbose=True
        )
        self.langchain_objects = [llm_math_chain]

    async def __call__(self, user_message: dict, ctx: RunContext):
        # Set up callback manager for the run
        callback_manager = CallbackManager([ConcatCallbackHandler(ctx)])
        for obj in self.other_langchain_objects + [
            self.agent_executor,
            self.agent_executor.agent.llm_chain,
            self.agent_executor.agent.llm_chain.llm,
        ]:
            obj.callback_manager = callback_manager

        # Run the agent
        await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(
            None, self.agent_executor.run, "".join(user_message["content_parts"])
        )
```

this looks good! one other way i was thinking of to implement this function could be the following:

```
def _reduce_tokens_below_limit(self, docs: List[Document]) -> List[Document]:

    num_docs = len(docs)
    
    if isinstance(self.combine_documents_chain, StuffDocumentsChain):
        tokens = [self.combine_documents_chain.llm_chain.llm.get_num_tokens(self._page_content(doc)) for doc in docs]

        while(sum(tokens[:num_docs])) > self.max_tokens_limit:
            num_docs -= 1

    return docs[:num_docs]
```
the advantage of this would be that we only have to call the `get_num_tokens` function once for each document, instead of multiple times. what do you think?
author: scadEfUr

body:
Sorry if this is a n00b question - just familiarizing myself here 😄 - but would it make sense to avoid the quadratic run time? 

```python
def _reduce_tokens_below_limit(self, docs: List[Document]) -> List[Document]:

    num_docs = len(docs)
    
    if isinstance(self.combine_documents_chain, StuffDocumentsChain):
        tokens = [self.combine_documents_chain.llm_chain.llm.get_num_tokens(self._page_content(doc)) for doc in docs]

        token_count = sum(tokens[:num_docs])
        while token_count > self.max_tokens_limit:
            num_docs -= 1
            token_count -= tokens[num_docs]

    return docs[:num_docs]
```
author: rowillia

body:
TL;DR: I'll make the change and go with what you recommend.

Though I wanted to share some thoughts on why I went with the recursive function design in the first place,

- This code was something I had implemented in my use case, so readability was crucial for me. I believe recursive code, when done right, is lovely.
- The K value would always be in single or lower double digits, so I didn't have to worry about Stack overflow or other performance issues. 
- E.g., Doing a similarity search in a vector store can be slow, primarily if you use a remote index like Pinecone; I wanted to call them sparingly. That's why I do slicing on the docs list.
- I felt encoding would add a few milliseconds, and given the OpenAI response is usually in higher single-digit seconds, it would be a premature optimization.

Despite that, we must focus on the most performant code when implementing this functionality in a general-purpose library. Because we won't know if K will remain a small value tomorrow for newer LLM models, and most likely, we will have moved to other things by then. 😄 

So thank you so much for taking the time for the suggestions.

**PS**: @rowillia: Yours is better, but the overhead is minimal when the K value is small, especially since addition is a quick operation. Doing encoding again and again, which was my original sin, is more expensive.

I agree with the name alignment being better! I was originally thinking about calling it "security" but realized that guards can do stuff that's not security related like verifying that an output is good (not just safe) 

Also, do you think it makes sense to change stuff from @RestrictionGuard to @AlignmentRestrict and so forth? I kinda think we should rename the module alignment to invite people to add more related tools but leave guards named the same because it's more descriptive and alignment is kinda a loaded term already.

I think we should have a separate section in "reference" called security. There we can talk about general stuff like .env and eval() safety for newcomers along with how to use guards/alignment tools specifically for safety.

body:
It looks to me like this may write the index memory pointer, i.e. the content of the variable, rather than the index content. This would manifest as a failure reading back the correct data from a newly launched process. There are index writing methods at https://github.com/facebookresearch/faiss/wiki/Index-IO,-cloning-and-hyper-parameter-tuning#generic-io-support .
EDIT: note also that faiss does support data larger than fits in ram, although langchain does not use this feature so it could be ignored. involves different saving/loading paradigms but generally uses disk files; see https://github.com/facebookresearch/faiss/wiki/Indexes-that-do-not-fit-in-RAM

body:
Im sorry I forgot to sync with @3coins on this before the first round of feedback. 

Unfortunately, like the feedback on rev1 about the input structure being specific to the model that you were/are using, this is also a bit specific to the output of the model that you were/are using. While I think a lot of models will return json, not all will. And even fewer will return json where the desired text is stored in a key `"generated_text"`. 

What do you think about making this a bit more generic like you did for the input? You could follow a similar pattern and allow for the user to pass a function that transforms raw bytes into a string object representing the model output?
author: sanjams2

body:
thanks for adding this! 

Im curious, is it considered more pythonic to pass functions around like this? In other languages (like Java) I know that people may opt to pass an object implementing an interface with some sort of `transform_input(str, Dict) -> bytes` function defined. I ask mostly out of curiosity about the decision. Given we may want to have a function on the output too, perhaps a single interface that defines a `transform_input` and `transform_output` could be an option? Then we could start defining common implementations of that interface for popular LLM models deployed onto SageMaker. I think it's good either way.

body: @nfcampos - It's a hassle to async-ify all vector store implementations so for now I'm running them in threadpool executor to avoid blocking main runloop. However, since vectorstore implementations are not guaranteed to be threadsafe, and with the default executor, you can have multiple threads, I'm not sure this is the best approach. Thoughts here?
author: agola11

body:
Hmm that's an interesting problem. The fundamental issue I think (but correct me if I'm wrong) is that, for maximum throughput, we want eg multiple simultaneous calls into this chain to each call into the vector store and 1. not block other things in the event loop and 2. not get blocked waiting on other vector store calls?

So if we somehow placed the vector store into its own dedicated thread (or something to that effect) that wouldn be enough to solve (1) but not (2) as multiple simultaneous calls to it would have to be queued up?

Looking at each vector store, the ones that do just do http requests I assume could be made async or threadsafe sync trivially. The ones that could be non-trivially not-thread safe are the ones that run locally, so faiss and chroma? Faiss looks to be thread safe for reads (not writes) https://github.com/facebookresearch/faiss/wiki/Threads-and-asynchronous-calls, don't know about chroma

Thoughts?
author: nfcampos

body: Yeah those are great points. Not many people are using the async api rn and getting this in is blocking some stuff we want to release tmrw so we'll probably merge by taking out the "run_in_executor" call to avoid any potential wonky behavior from thread interactions.

body:
After attempting to implement this I've run into a question. Currently the implementation is invoked as follows:
```
_id = 'testing_new_class'
table_name = 'ConversationTable'
memory = ConversationBufferDynamoDBMemory(id=_id, table_name=table_name) 
```

Yet under the class you suggest I assume it would have to be invoked like this instead:

```
session_id = 'testing_new_class'
table_name = 'ConversationTable'
buffer = DynamoDBBuffer(table_name)
memory = ServerlessMemory(buffer=buffer, session_id=session_id)
```

Just confirming that this is the implementation you have in mind.
author: KBB99

body: Nevermind, I've assumed this is what you mean and I think it is better so future databases from Azure, GCP, etc. can be easily added as alternate buffer sources while making use of the same ServerlessMemory construct. body:

That being said, if it's a ton of extra work, I can refactor.

body:
Hey @stepp1, glad you found my API wrapper! You're right: it's unofficial, but it's actively maintained (and pretty stable).

I have a few suggestions for shortening the `ArXivSearchAPIWrapper` code; I haven't tested them, but I think they should be safe to drop-in. These updates make more direct use of `arxiv`'s built-in generator and result classes (removing an intermediate).

I'll leave it to @hwchase17 and other maintainers to weigh in on whether the wrapper follows typical langchain patterns. Happy to re-review here if you tag me.

In my personal experimentation, I've been using a *very* slim decorated tool:

```python
@tool
def arxiv_search(query: str) -> str:
    """Search arXiv for academic papers relevant the input query."""
    summaries = []
    for result in arxiv.Search(query=query, max_results=10).results():
        summaries.append("{title}\nDate: {date}\nAuthors: {authors}\nSummary: {summary}".format(
            title=result.title,
            date=result.published,
            authors=", ".join([a.name for a in result.authors]),
            summary=result.summary
        ))
    return "\n\n".join(summaries)
```

This obscures a lot of client configuration, but for single-page results sets (when `max_results` ≤ the client's `page_size`, which defaults to 100) the client configuration essentially doesn't matter.
author: lukasschwab

body:
It's nice to have a helper folding the `self` parameters into a search, but I'd leave it up to the caller when/how to resolve the generator.

```suggestion
    def _search(self, query: str) -> Iterator:
        """Construct an arXiv search for query with stored parameters."""
        from arxiv import Search
        return Search(
            query=query,
            max_results=self.max_results,
            sort_by=self.sort_by,
            sort_order=self.sort_order
        )
```
author: lukasschwab

body:
I think you can eliminate much of this code by using the `Search(...).results()` generator directly, rather than wrapping it in a generator (`_parse_results`) converting the results from objects to maps.

If you *do* want to do that conversion, you might consider using the `__dict__ ` built-in: `result. __dict__ `.

Note: this rewrite of `run` assumes the suggestion I made below in this file (`_arxiv_search_results` → `_search`).

```suggestion
def run(self, query: str) -> str:
        """Run query through ArXiv Search and parse result."""
        # papers = self.results(query)

        papers = []
        for paper in self._search(query).results():
            papers.append("\n".join([
                f'Title: {paper.title.strip()}',
                f'Authors: {", ".join([a.name for a in paper.authors])}',
                f'Abstract: {paper.summary.strip()}'
            ]))

        if not papers:
            return "No good arXiv Search result was found"
        
        return "\n\n".join(papers)
```
author: lukasschwab

body:
Love to see this. Thanks for doing this as this was definitely on @tylerhutcherson and I's list. I have a couple high level comments.

1. I think the name ``Redis`` would be better since people might assume ``RediSearch`` is a different thing. First part of the connection could use the ``INFO MODULES`` command to ensure that RediSearch is loaded. Another point is that we allow for vectors to be indexed in RedisJSON which would be confusing if people used the RediSearch container to use both modules without knowing they had to use the Redis-Stack container for this.

2. In relation, I think it would be nice to also have the ability to store as hash or JSON. This, I can do in a follow up PR though.

3. I left some performance pieces in there, but I'm happy to also do these as a follow up.

4. Lots of points on connection arguments. It would be ideal to match the semantics of the REdis-py library so that this is a similar drop in for our community.

5. I didn't see the addition of a optional package like ``pip install langchain[redis]`` which I think would be really nice. pinning a version to ensure these features are present is a must though.

Let me know what you think, we are happy to contribute some of these changes if you get stumped. Please reach out for clarification. 

Awesome!

Not sure what that means for the usefulness of FewShotChatPromptTemplate as a template though, beyond inserting text into the final `HumanMessage`.

Separately, could probably also define sensible defaults for example_input/output, given most examples don't need to be wrapped with anything extra there. (And increases the utility of the final answer, since the final text "suffix" isn't likely to be wrapped in the same way.

Suffix also feels weird, since that's where the importance of the _actual question being asked_ goes, right? Is that just legacy of what it was called historically? Wondering if there's a better name for "the actual data input this prompt attempts to answer for you"

body:
Conversational bots tend to include extra verbiage "Here you go: XXX" or "XXX. That does what you want, but lemme know if you want to change it". And so you can ask the chatbot to include it in markdown/xml, and it might do "Here you go: <result>A,B,C</result>". So can perhaps be done with RegexParser, but may want to feed RegexParser into CommaSeparatedListOutput when dealing with conversational responses. Maybe it's not worth chaining outputparsers here, but sharing one of the differences for chatbots.

Note that proper few-shot-prompting can solve this and make the chatbots less chatty, so maybe this isn't as important if done well.

I tried doing this in the class at first but the SQLAlchemy models (CollectionStore and EmbeddingStore) are evaluated at import time and needs the Vector import from pgvector. I even tried adding the models inside the PGVector class under ```__post_init__```, but it was causing issue while running the integration tests as the classes were reinitialized and sqlalchemy doesn't like it. 

This is the reason i have added 
```python
from langchain.vectorstores.pgvector import PGVector
```
instead of 
```python
from langchain.vectorstores import PGVector
```
in the docs to avoid import errors.

body:
I'd suggest thinking about how this relates to the self-critique+revision approach from anthropic that someone already added: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/1147

This `RestrictionGuard` is similar in spirit, but at least 2 downsides:

- it's redundant/overlapping
- it doesn't allow for revision/retry logic

Meanwhile, `ConstitutionalChain` may have some downsides too. eg at first glance, it's `n` LLM calls to evaluate `n` constitutional principles which is not tenable for real/heavy use case. 


Generally tho this is cool! Models evaluating themselves / other models. There's an online validation (+ retry) use case + offline evaluation use case. Should try to converge on something and then write many more examples. Happy to help with.
author: jerwelborn

body:
I agree the Anthropic approach is actually better than what I have implemented for the restriction guard when using it on LLMS. That being said, I think of this as being a separate tool that overlaps in that one area.

The restriction guard can be applied to any function that has text as an output. This means it can be applied to agents, to llms, to chains, or even to user input. It should be seen as a blanket way to restrict text using an LLM- not just a way to restrict LLMs. For example, we may want to use an LLM to restrict a user from asking anything that is off-topic. This is impossible to do with a ConstitutionalChain for obvious reasons. I actually thought about adding more complex retry logic and was planning on it at first but it would prevent this type of use.

One solution could be to edit the docs to make very clear the tradeoffs here when choosing one or the other for LLM guarding. The ConstitutionalChain ought to be the recommended way of doing things for chains. For things like checking user input, agent output, even stuff like cleaning data (maybe you want to remove all fake sounding submitted emails before adding them to your newsletter) the restriction guard is useful.

One other things to note is that the other two guards, @StringGuard and @CustomGuard, are also useful as distinct from the constitutional chain. In particular, the custom guard can be used to pass in any sentiment model. For a heavy use case I anticipate this being far more cost effective than a constitutional chain or restriction guard. A user can train a local sentiment model on the type of response they want to see and run it instead of multiple calls to gpt. See the bottom of security.ipynb for a simple example.

At the end of the day, happy to help change stuff or move it around, but I do think this stuff should be seen as a different set of tools for an overlapping but different set of uses than the constitutional chain. 

Also, though, the prompt for the restriction guard can definitely use some work and wherever we can borrow common logic / code from the constitutional chain (while retaining the llm agnostic approach of the restriction guard: eg no retry logic) we should.

body:
Yeah, although with logic inverted -- allow folks to specify what exceptions can be excepted if they want to rather than have to throw a specific exception for halting execution.

In my mind though AssertionErrors are special and generally not be caught as they indicate there's a logical error in the code and execution should halt.

The use case is to make sure that we don't hide a logical error in the parsing code. So when an exception is raised we can distinguish between parser input being malformed vs. the parser itself having a bug.

I imagine with the agents, things could get interesting as they may try to compensate for the parse bug
author: eyurtsev

body:
How are you thinking about the scope of responsibility of an OutputGuardrail?

1) Take string output
2) Decode it (e.g., json decode)
3) Validate it
4) Any additional control logic? 
   -- e.g., logic to accept/reject the output if it looks like it contains bad language?
   -- retry logic on failure (i assume that should be at a higher level?)

----

Do you have a sense of how/why an InputGuardRail be different? (with the exception of potentially steps (1) and (2) being optional?)

Agree with what I think @eyurtsev is getting at above:

The scope of OutputGuardrail/OutputParserGuardrail/whatever not super clear and seems not required right now: supposing Guardrail means some encapsulation of a validation/verification step + optional retry step. actually the Parser already raises validation error. and then the retry here will be very generic (likely, need to re-run the full forward pass again.) here's an example how this would look: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/6628230a8a527b3f75abc529ecf80c5d6071b855/parsing_guardrails.ipynb
author: jerwelborn

body:
How are various forms of validation + retry incorporated. It seems we these 2 cases. Pseudocode:

```
def predict(prompt):
  completion = llm(prompt)
  try:
    parsed_completion = parser(completion)
  except ParserException as e:
    # Handle failures captured by ECF
    if retry_parse:
      retry_completion = generic_retry_thing(prompt, completion)
      parsed_completion = parser(retry_completion)

  completion = parsed_completion
  
  for guardrail in guardrails:
    # Handle failures captured by guardrail
    completion = guardrail.evaluate(prompt, completion)

  return completion
```

body:
- i guess this is "comma ok" from go, which is a nice idiom
- the EAFP/pythonic way is probably to raise an GuardrailException
- the flow control anywhere guardrails are used then will look like

```
try:
  evaluation = guardrail.evaluation(prompt, completion) # keep GuardrailEvaluation, easier to extend later
  if evaluation.revised_completion:
    completion = evaluation.revised_completion
except GuardrailException as e:
  raise e or something
```
 
- seems like the caller has to know a lot about this guardrail concept. for example, a completion may not initially satisfy the guardrail till after fixing/retrying the completion.. we have to check `evaluate`'s return value to know.

Wdyt is best?

author: zoltan-fedor

body: There are too many storage options, for example different databases, filesystems, cloud storage etc. These classes are not core to this project, so not sure if they belong here. Adding examples to the documentation should suffice for now, if there is a need for these in future, we can always add them in a separate PR, leaving them out is not a one-way door.
author: 3coins

body:
I agree that there are too many storage options, but I don't agree that that means that we should not include any here.

Based on that same argument of having too many options what is the reason to have any functionality in this library at all? We could just say that prompt engineering has too many options and just write a documentation on how one can write applications with prompt engineering instead of writing `langchain`.

It makes sense to write things into the library that is expected to be used by many, so they don't all need to re-implement the wheel.

But yes, for those storage options which are very niche, the argument not to include them could be valid.

Redis is definitely not niche, so that should be included but DynamoDB could be considered as niche and not included.

Ps.: I would be curious as to how many users of this library will be able to write their own driver for their own storage option. I would guess that probably less than 30%. :-)

Either way this decision (what to include in the library and what not to) probably belongs to @hwchase17.
author: zoltan-fedor

body:
```suggestion
    """Base interface for chat message history
    See `ChatMessageHistory` for default implementation.
    """
    
    """
    Example:
        .. code-block:: python

            class FileChatMessageHistory(BaseChatMessageHistory):
                storage_path:  str
                session_id: str
               
               @property
               def messages(self):
                   with open(os.path.join(storage_path, session_id), 'r:utf-8') as f:
                       messages = json.loads(f.read())
                    return messages_from_dict(messages)     
                
               def add_user_message(self, message: str):
                   message_ = HumanMessage(content=message)
                   messages = self.messages.append(_message_to_dict(_message))
                   with open(os.path.join(storage_path, session_id), 'w') as f:
                       json.dump(f, messages)
               
               def add_ai_message(self, message: str):
                   message_ = AIMessage(content=message)
                   messages = self.messages.append(_message_to_dict(_message))
                   with open(os.path.join(storage_path, session_id), 'w') as f:
                       json.dump(f, messages)
                       
               def clear(self):
                   with open(os.path.join(storage_path, session_id), 'w') as f:
                       f.write("[]")
    """    

    messages: List[BaseMessage]
```

body:
@hwchase17, @tylerhutcherson ,
What happened to the TTL ([see](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/2122/commits/dd72715410fca61de1e7bcebe0b4551ddba04ceb#diff-7ef42aa53f31305f6c6ea1c5e94084f5164f274e6de1575322e6e61dad018138L72))?
I am fine changing to using a Redis List data type, but it seems we have also dropped the TTL option with this change.

I don't think it is a good idea not to have a TTL, as that means that these records would be kept in Redis forever with no expiration option.

Outside it probably not being a good idea to accumulate data forever in Redis, also most data privacy laws disallow that.

With `lpush` now you would need to set the expiration of the list separately, each time in the append operation,
see https://redis-py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/commands.html#redis.commands.cluster.RedisClusterCommands.expire
author: zoltan-fedor

body:
We should add back the TTL option which got removed by switching to using the Redis list object - as the `lpush` has no TTL option, now the TTL need to be set by a separate call, see https://redis-py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/commands.html#redis.commands.cluster.RedisClusterCommands.expire


Something like
```
if self.ttl:
    self.redis_client.expire(self.key, self.ttl)
```
author: zoltan-fedor

body: @zoltan-fedor good catch, added back in
author: hwchase17

body: Good catch
author: tylerhutcherson

body: This is true. Though managing the size of that list could be simple, and even configurable. One mode of operation could be maintaining the list at a **fixed size.** That combined with overall conversation TTL should be sufficient.
author: tylerhutcherson

body:
@hwchase17, @tylerhutcherson , @3coins,
I know this just got released, but trying it out just found a bug with it.

Unfortunately the use of `self.redis_client.lrange(self.key, 0, -1)` returns the messages in the list in a RANDOM ORDER, not in the order how it was inserted.

I haven't yet looked into how to fix this, just wanted to let everybody know. 

CORRECTION: `lrange()` seems to receive the messages in descending order (last added message first), so it seems we just need to sort them in the opposite order, that should be an easy fix. I will make a new PR for that.
See [PR raised](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/2167) to fix this.

body:
Added and also had this documented a bit below. 

Composing/chaining is a reasonable idea... 

I started with planner as being responsible for generating a plan as well as invoking the execution, i.e. it was an agent. There were too many instructions stuffed into the prompt so I split it out. However having an agent at the top-level is nice... it can generate a plan, try to execute it, have errors bubble up and re-plan. Not that I do this in any repeatable, sophisticated way atm (although often it "just happens"), but it's a natural next step.

body:
Exposing a generator on the callbackhandler via `stream` will work, but the main issue is that with the current API, we don't have control over which LLM will stream tokens. Several chains (like `ConversationalRetrieval`) make multiple calls to the llm, and we'd only want to stream back the results from the last one. I think there are couple things we can do:

1) Add in the `StreamCallbackHandler` that @nfcampos introduced in this diff, but remove the llm parameter in the constructor to the `LangChainPoeHandler` class, replacing with the callback handler. The set-up for streaming is done outside of the `PoeHandler` as it will vary chain to chain. It would look something like this (for `ConversationalRetrievalQA`):
```
# Construct a ConversationalRetrievalChain with a streaming llm for combine docs
# and a separate, non-streaming llm for question generation
llm = OpenAI(temperature=0)
stream_handler = StreamCallbackHandler()
streaming_llm = OpenAI(streaming=True, 
callback_manager=AsyncCallbackManager([stream_handler]), verbose=True, temperature=0)

question_generator = LLMChain(llm=llm, prompt=CONDENSE_QUESTION_PROMPT)
doc_chain = load_qa_chain(streaming_llm, chain_type="stuff", prompt=QA_PROMPT)

qa = ConversationalRetrievalChain(
    retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(), combine_docs_chain=doc_chain, question_generator=question_generator)

poe_handler = LangChainPoeHandler(qa, stream_handler)
```
In `LangChainPoeHandler.get_response`:
```
async def get_response(self, query):
  run = asyncio.create_task(self.chain.acall(query))

  async for token in this.callback_handler.stream():
      yield token

  await run
```
We can have an `as_poe_handler` to some of the common chains to cut down on this boilerplate.

2) Second, more involved approach which is something we probably don't want to do now but will want to revisit in the future is to have the option to return a generator for tokens on `BaseChain` via a method called `stream` (which is what we have for some LLMs already). Initially, when we implemented streaming, we used callbacks bc it was the easiest solution and didn't require any API changes to chains/etc. However, some users might want more flexibilty and the ability to interact with the raw generator.

body: is this needed if we are also tracking the time_elapsed in the _should_continue method?
author: hwchase17

body: _should_continue only checks after each iteration is completed.  Suppose you wanted a time limit of 30s but each iteration takes ~5s.  If an iteration finishes at 29s, _should_continue will return True, another iteration will run, and the loop will terminate after ~34s.  With asyncio_timeout, execution will be interrupted after exactly 30s

def dp_filter(x: Dict, filter: Optional[Dict[str, str]] = None) -> bool:

body:
It may be nice to separate the single doc and the batch doc interfaces

```python

class BaseDocumentProcessor(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def process_document(self, documents: Document) -> Document:
        """Process documents."""

    def process_documents(self, documents: List[Document]) -> List[Document]:
      return [self.process_document(doc) for doc in documents] # <-- This is annoying to implement all the time
```

This would provide a default for loop implementation while allowing inherting classes to over-ride if batching can be made more efficient. 

Batch composition is easy: 

```python
class  SequentialBatchProcessor():  # <-- Only supports batch case, not streaming
   def __init__(self, processors: Sequence[Processors]) -> None:
      self.processors = []

    def process_documents(self, documents: List[Document] | Document) -> List[Document]:
      docs = docs if isinstance(docs, (list, tuple)) else [docs]
      for processor in self.processors:
       docs = processor(docs)
       return docs
```

IMO would be nice to: 
* swap `List` -> `Sequence` as well on inputs
* figure out if it's possible to make a better streaming version (more complicated given that a splitter can be used as a processor with interface of doc -> [doc] or [doc] -> [doc])
author: eyurtsev

author: vowelparrot

body:
Thanks for your review. `RequestsGetToolWithParsing` creates a new `OpenAI` with the default `model_name` on instantiation (https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/issues/2546#issuecomment-1505136535), so I'm just recreating that same behavior but with the correct class when an `Azure` llm is being used. That llm instance will have the `engine` parameter, which is necessary to call the OpenAI API on an Azure service (as it is now, the call fails).

>we shouldn't be handling individual LLM providers in this file

The original code is creating instances of `OpenAI` so it already handles specific providers. I wonder then if it's necessary to do some additional refactoring, so that particular providers don't show at all here.
author: jnmarti

body:
I'm less familiar with the AzureOpenAI endpoint in particular, but what about the following:


In the `planner.py` , update llm_chain to be

```python
def _get_default_get_llm_chain():
     return LLMChain(
        llm=OpenAI(),
        prompt=PARSING_GET_PROMPT,
    )
class RequestsGetToolWithParsing(BaseRequestsTool, BaseTool):
    name = "requests_get"
    description = REQUESTS_GET_TOOL_DESCRIPTION
    response_length: Optional[int] = MAX_RESPONSE_LENGTH
    llm_chain: LLMChain =  Field(default_factory=_get_default_get_llm_chain)
...
 ```

And something similar in the other tools.

Then here, if we remove the`_get_llm_chain()` method and instead in the `_create_api_controller_agent` method instantiate a chain as

```python
  llm_chain LLMChain(llm=llm, prompt=PARSING_POST_PROMPT)
```

then pass that directly.

Would that work for the AzureOpenAI llm?


The original defaulting to OpenAI is an oversight, so this keeps most of the backwards compatibility without forcing people to use davinci 3

body:
Yeah we can definitely move the `BlobParser`, we'll need to decide what's the most logical place

One thing I wasn't so sure about is that the document loaders right now are basically content fetchers + parsers and the majority of their work should be focused on parsing, so it felt like a parser abstraction could be accommodated at this level of the hierarchy. I'll start diffing smaller more careful changes now, and we can iron out all the namespaces to make sure everything is in the most logical place. 

`base` aka `typedefs` ideally don't import much at all (except for other typedefs), so there's that...

IMO it's generally OK to import more nested code, since the nested code is owned by the importing code. The dangerous imports are when reaching into parent paths or sibling paths since that means that we're reaching into nested code that doesn't belong to the given module/package

body: I think the issue is slightly larger. for most VectorStore classes embedding_function seems to be of type Embeddings, and then the vectorstore class handles calling either self.embedding_function.embed_documents (takes list as input) or self.embedding_function.embed_query (takes single str). here embedding_function is of type Callable, so you're forced to pass in one of embed_documents or embed_query. current implementation seems to assume you passed in embed_query. Think we should just change type of embedding_function -> Embeddings and then call embed_documents / embed_query as needed. Can look at most other VectorStores for an example (e.g. `ChromaVectorStore`)

body:
The current behavior is to re-use a Comet Experiment if one was created before and if not create a new one.
That behavior can be confusing for users that creates multiple instance of `CometCallbackHandler` and can leads to subtle bugs where the new Experiment is in the wrong workspace or project_name.
So we decided to get with a more simpler approach, one instance of `CometCallbackHandler` equals one instance of Comet Experiment.

body:
`np.where(arr == 0)` returns a tuple of arrays containing the indices where the condition `arr == 0` is satisfied. This is a tuple with one element, which is an array containing the index where the condition is true (where the value is 0). 

To get the actual index value where the condition is true, we use indexing with `[0][0]`. The first `[0]` selects the first element of the tuple (which is the array with the index where the condition is true), and the second `[0]` selects the first (and only) element of that array. So `np.where(arr == 0)[0][0]` will return the index of the first occurrence of `0` in the array.

author: Ryangr0

body: the base `VectorStore` class does not explicitly expose the MMR algorithms parameter lambda as an argument. The Chroma implementation for example ignores lambda and the underlying MMR algorithm is always invoked with the default value. Because lamda is an important parameter of how the MMR works I have decided to make it tweakable by clients of this VectorStore implementation. But now there is an inconsistency whereby some implementation expose lambda and some don't. This is a problems for clients that want the same functionality (in this case the ability to tweak the lambda value) across different VectorStores.
author: cs0lar

body:
@vowelparrot In [the issue that implemented loras in llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp/pull/820) they say "Using --lora disables mmap since the models have to be modified anyway." llama-cpp-python diverges from this behavior by allowing both, which is what causes the segfault. I [just made an issue in llama-cpp-python about this](https://github.com/abetlen/llama-cpp-python/issues/107).

I could probably remove this logic entirely and trust that llama-cpp-python will patch this and make the issue go away. I'm going to bed now, but in the morning I'll either remove these lines or add a comment and raise a ValueError instead.

body:
If the embedding function isn't normalized, this could result in a score thats outside the "relevance score" bounds (0, 1).  This is fine for the `similarity_search_with_score` method itself since the score is unitless / not interpreted. 

We could add a function to `langchain/math_utils`

```python
def scaled_cosine_similarity(X: Matrix, Y: Matrix) -> np.ndarray:
    """Cosine similarity scaled to the range [0, 1]."""
    return (cosine_similarity(X, Y) + 1) / 2
```

And then call here:
```
scaled_cosine_similarity([res["_additional"]["vector"]], [self._embedding.embed_query(query)])[0]
```

body:
double brackets is how you escape brackets for python str formatting

{ = formatted first time you call .format, {{ = formatted second time, {{{{ = formatted third time. this string will be added to overall prompt template and then format will be called twice (once to add the description of the doc store and metadata fields, once to enter the actual user query), so that by the time this is passed to llm it'll be reduced to a single { (so it'll look like json)

so 
```python
x = "{{{{foo_bar}}}}"
x.format() == "{{foo_bar}}"
x.format().format() == "{foo_bar}"
x.format().format().format(foo_bar="whatever") == "whatever"
```
author: dev2049

1. it can be slower and harder to scale. Once you do it for many modules, you can end up writing and deleting files hundreds of times, every time you run the unit tests.

3. temp files tests are harder to write and maintain

body: What's the motivation for using the apply/aapply in a first loop then zipping vs. the original structure (and using predict_and_parse / apredict_and_parse)?
author: vowelparrot

body:
@vowelparrot As mentioned in the PR, I basically copied the implementation from the `MapReduceDocumentsChain`. As far as I understand, the `apply`/`aapply` methods are specifically created to apply chain operations to sequences or multiple inputs/documents, which is exactly the case here. 

Thus, imho there are two advantages of this change:

- The implementations for `compress_documents` and `acompress_documents` are basically identical which increases maintainability for changes to sync/async operations in the future.
- due to the similar syntax, it will be easier to wrap some existing CombineDocumentsChain for LLMChainExtractor instead of maintaining a separate DocumentCompressor implementation (but that is an architetctural decision).

But if you prefer that, I can revert the sync changes and just add the async implementation; this is my first contribution so  happy to get guidance.....

Two suggestions:

1. Introduce a common type hierarchy for "operators" / "directives" whatever we choose to call them. So we don't have to do `List[Union[Comparison, Operation]]`, but instead can do `Sequence[Directive]`.
2. Could we remove everything from the schema file except for the schema, so it's easy to see the type hierarchy?

Suggestion to favor `Sequence` over `List` on inputs -- `Sequences` accept tuples/lists and the collection is immutable

def format_attribute_info(info: Sequence[AttributeInfo]) -> str:

body: What do you think about using SIGNED_INT and SIGNED_FLOAT to distinguish between int vs. float at least at the level of the grammar? We could do additional coercion as needed depending on the schema of the data that we're querying, but being able to avoid it may help us avoid some floating point bugs potentially
author: eyurtsev

body:
nit: `Sequence` handles both list and tuples types and marks the container as immutable. I generally like using `Sequence` for inputs in functions (so the function implementation promises not to mutate the collection), and using `List` for return types of functions (to give the caller the right to mutate the output if they so choose to)

```suggestion
        allowed_operators: Optional[Sequence[Operator]],
```

# or, if they only occur at start and end...
a = a.strip('"')
'sajdkasjdsak" "asdasdasds'

# or, if they only occur at start...
a = a.lstrip('"')

# or, if they only occur at end...

```

body:
I know this was in part trying to maintain the existing code but it does feel a bit strange to turn the parsed information into a string only to turn it back into json. I think in general what's different about your change and mine is that mine really changes the goal of this function - trying to determine the LLM's intent. So mine doesn't throw any exceptions anymore that aren't handled within this function, and the Agent should always receive an action or finish no matter what.

My PR: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/3480

body:
Yeah, got you! Thank you for sharing the example! This is just my personal idea, please let me know if this doesn’t make sense to you. :)

In the example you shared, the `kwargs` is passed to the class itself. So we can consider this function as an entry to initiate this class itself. Code below:
https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/72c5c15f7fdc1918880e3cfd0949199e5a0b5bda/langchain/chains/qa_with_sources/base.py#L39-L64

But in the PR here, the parameter `kwargs` is used as input for LLMChain which is a intermediate layer between the function and the class. From my understanding, a more suitable solution is to build another entry function which build this extractor from LLMChain directly, which, however, is overlapping with the default creation method of this class. That’s why I think it’s easier to create a LLMChain outside and then build this class directly.

Please let me know if this makes sense to you. Thank you!

body:
yeah, so there are requirements of these two values. They could be invalid in some cases. I think there are several places where we can place this check. Intuitively, the earlier the better, which points to the class initiation process.

In `pydantic`, which we’re using here, it has a concept of validator, which can provide certain assertions during the process of initiation. With a validator, we can validator both `blocksize` and `blockcount` at the same time and raise exceptions or auto-fix? or silently fallback to default.

reference and examples in https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/usage/validators/

body: Hi @saginawj , may I share my thoughts with you here? From my experience, it’s usually easier to debug or read when we split the logic flow earlier. Applying the learning here, it might be a good idea to do this `self.get_all_tokens == True` check before running the request. In this way, maybe we can move the old logics into a separate function, check `get_all_tokens`, when it’s false, fallback to the original flow, otherwise, follow the new flow. Plz lmk if this makes sense to you. Thank you!

author: skcoirz

body: `@staticmethod`? :)
author: skcoirz

body:
@skcoirz I appreciate the feedback!  I'm certainly open to suggestions on how to improve the flow of logic here.  The reason I put this self.get_all_tokens check after the first API call (which I agree is maybe a bit counter-intuitive) is because the tokenId can take many formats (e.g. int, hex with leading zeros, etc.) and that format is needed for the 'get_all' call (else it will return an empty array).  So I execute the first API call, and if self.get_all_tokens is true, I determine the tokenId type and use it to paginate through the API.  With either flow, that first call is needed.  If the flag is false, the loop stops after the first call but if true it will continue until no tokens are returned from the API.  

I hope that's clear.  I can certainly change this flow if the logic is unclear.  I just wanted to outline the thinking behind my thought process. Thanks again!
author: saginawj

body: thanks @skcoirz  :).  will fix in next commit
author: saginawj

body: Open to the concept- but want to make sure I understand your thinking.  Are you suggesting we exit the loop if the set time is exceeded, or only add the time check for testing purposes?
author: saginawj

body:
Yeah I think it would be convenient to provide users a way to early stop based on time check. This could help us mitigate very extreme cases when the expected time cost is very high.

A similar idea is in use in `AgentExecutor` where a single user request may requires multiple rounds of immediate searching. Code pointer below. I think it’s a good idea. Maybe we can consider something similar here?

https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/agents/agent.py#L830

body:
thanks for the feedback.  I'll take another look at this, but unfortunately not until later this week.   The good news is that I think there are 3 approaches:

- the one currently in blockchain.py uses a max_execution_time param that the client can set if 40secs is too long to wait.  I think from a client perspective, this will be ok for most people.
- fixing the async.  currently, it is 'working' in that the test cases are passing, but it is not executing in parallel correctly.  so the request takes too long.
- at some point today, I had the solution 'working' but the requests were failing because they were being sent to the API too quickly and I was hitting rate limits.  Unfortunately, I moved off that approach too quickly and need to remember how I had this setup.  There must be a way to batch or slow-down those requests to prevent the rate limit issue.

body:
We generally want to avoid default arguments that are mutable.


```python
def func_with_mutable(some_value, some_dict={}):
    some_dict[some_value] = some_value
    return some_dict

print(func_with_mutable(1))  # Output: {1: 1}
print(func_with_mutable(2))  # Output: {1: 1, 2: 2}, not {2: 2} as you might expect
```

body: hi @mbchang , curious about your thoughts here. This seems asking the bot to bias toward using the tool as much as possible but in real life, I think there is a balance between external-tool-empowered answers and self-generated answers. Wondering if you have any good idea how to balance here? Is there any paper related to this? Thank you so much!
author: skcoirz

body:
You could specify a service that you would like to have access to , in your development environment.

For example, if you are using milvus vectordb, then you can specify that in the docker compose file like this:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/129657162/236110016-2f606ebd-b120-4428-b2dc-f4ef2bbde4df.png)

vscode or codespaces will bring that service as well when loading the repository code and make that available in dev environment. So there will two containers:

- vscode container hosting the repository code and acts as the backend for vscode frontend.
- Another container which has the service you want to use. 

Vscode and Service container needs to be on the same network so that service can be made available to vscode container using port forwarding.

It is really helpful in quickly bringing up dev environments for developing new features, enhancing features, bringing up dependent service for development with less hassle, you would just need the image of the service you want to bring up.

We could store multiple docker compose files for different use cases.

body:
No. We don't need to change test data. Thanks.

> hmmm in theory i like this, but the distinction seems a bit blurred. for example, why is Google Drive a formatter and not a knowledge document loader

I'll add more description to this. 
The idea is "knowledge loader" works with storage that we do not control. Something that can be used as a "tool" (in terms of LangChain). That can be accessed with queries. Something that can be considered as a source of "external" knowledge. We can allow LLM to make queries and get information or we can download documents and use them in a more controllable way.
"Formatters" can be as easy as transformers for CSV, SQL, etc. But they also can be cloud services or app stores. They can be hosted out of our control but the information inside is under our control.

body:
I like the idea of multithreading as it’s a good way to speed up. What I’m not sure are several potential conflicts
- Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) only allows 1 thread at a time. For it, maybe multi-processing is preferred.
- Resource usage is increased. I don’t know where users would use LangChain, when the CPU resource is limited, this can be a concern. They can choose not to use it thorough.

Overall, I think it’s a strategical question to LangChain of whether to support multi-threading/processing. Will wait for maintainers to answer as they have a better view of the big picture.
author: skcoirz

body:
Thank you for the comment!

- Yes I agree about GIL. We have one thread running but there is an exception: waiting for I/O operations. When one thread waits for file to be loaded other can operate. This is the main usage of the concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor.

- Multithreading is optional and will probably by used only by people who will discover the need. As the one person who requested this originaly.

body:
Hello @triet-lq-holistics , right! The use case I had in mind when implementing the JSONLoader was that only fields containing text are considered valid. But some use cases may very well require storing a full subset of the JSON data, and I think we should support that.

However, I think that the behavior of converting arbitrary non-text data as input to the `page_content` should be made explicit by the user. For example, exposing an argument like `text_content: bool = True` that must be checked in the initial set of validators could be useful.

```Python
if text_content and not isinstance(content, str):
    raise ValueError(f"Expected page_content is string, got {type(content)} instead. Set `text_content=False` if the desired input for `page_content` is not a string.")
````

The reason why this could be useful is because `jq` has a very permissive syntax. And so, a user may unintentionally store non-text content if we don't have this in place.

wdyt, @dev2049 ?

body:
@baskaryan this could be a good candidate of a class to rewrite using the blob generators

Single blob generator takes in its interface:

- auth or settings for auth
- scopes

filters:
- drive
- object ids and/or folder
- file extensions
- chunk_size -- for large file downloads
- max file size -- limit to file size
- show progress (bool) -- progress indicator

it yields Blobs

---

Then can be combined with Generic Loader which will allow decoupling the hard-coding between parsers and content fetching

this can be a challenge to auto tests. During CI process, each time a new "virtual machine" is created with our predefined env setup and dependencies before running our auto tests here. The assumption here is that there is a local mongoDB host service (and several env variables), which is not true in auto test environment.

to make it work, 
- we will need to provide the whole setup in github CI workflow config, the dependencies will need to be installed too. (interesting, I can only see unit_test here. @vowelparrot / @dev2049 , do you know where are integration_tests configured? Thanks) (unittest config: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/.github/workflows/test.yml)
- alternatively, we can make a mock mongodb API with predefined answer for each specific request.

body:
The parent class defines a `load` method which does not allow args or kwargs.


```suggestion
    def load(self) -> List[Document]:
```

kwargs can be provided from the __ init __ instead.

The reason to keep the load method consistent is to follow the following rule of thumb for sub-classes: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liskov_substitution_principle

This loader looks a lot like the pymypdf loader. Is pdfplumber using pymupdf under the hood?
author: eyurtsev

body: extract_words and extract_text share some arguments, but I think you are right, we would want them separated. However, after searching a bit it seems like you cannot pass 2 separate kwargs with `def func(**kwargs1, **kwargs2)`. Is there a better convention for doing this?

body:
I think decoupling the parsing from loading is good for the loaders in https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/4356/files, but for `annotate_and_load` it can be problematic. After using pdfplumber for a bit it looks like the parsed results can be different from that of PyMuPDF, and I worry that whichever parser used by user can be unrepresented in the visualized output of pdfplumber. Hence, the coupled `annotate_and_load` to force users to only use pdfplumber as parser if they would like to utilize the visual debugger.

As for a better interface, as mentioned above with @dev2049, I am trying to figure out a way to pass **kwargs to multiple destinations. Please let me know if there is a best convention. I will separate the kwargs for `extract_words`, `extract_text`, and `to_image`.
author: lesterpjy

body:
pdfplumber looks to be build on [pdfminer.six](https://github.com/pdfminer/pdfminer.six), and the documentation provides a overview of [comparisons](https://github.com/jsvine/pdfplumber#comparison-to-other-libraries) with other PDF parsing libraries.

For passing the kwargs, I thought we can update class dictionary with `self.__dict__.update(kwargs)` after `__init__` with **kwargs, but then the different methods of pdfplumber would not be able to access without explicit `get` on the class dictionary. And the kwargs can get extensive...
```
.extract_words(x_tolerance=3, y_tolerance=3, keep_blank_chars=False, use_text_flow=False, horizontal_ltr=True, vertical_ttb=True, extra_attrs=[], split_at_punctuation=False, expand_ligatures=True)
```
Again, maybe there's a better way.
author: lesterpjy

body:
Hello, @EandrewJones! :wave:  This is awesome! 

How difficult would it be to expand the test to `k=2` and have a meaningful difference for the 2nd retrieved result between the distance metrics?

The main concern is that with `k=1`, the test doesn't verify that the distance metric works correctly, only that the vector store can be instantiated with the given distance metric value. So a future refactor, could accidentally break this feature.
author: eyurtsev

body:
Sure, thing. I can put something together with `k=2` for each metric showing the outputs and scores are as expected.

Though if the distance metrics don't work correctly that would imply an issue with the RedisStack implementation. We cannot directly address that on the langchain side apart from maybe excluding metrics that don't work correctly.
author: EandrewJones

body:
Yeah you're right -- redis is almost certainly doing the right thing!

I am mostly worried about future proofing against an accidental refactor on the python side that would affect the plumbing

e.g., if during a refactor this were to happen (say someone was debugging something and commits the code by mistake)

```python

instance._create_index(dim=len(embeddings[0]), distance_metric=distance_metric)
```

to which ignores the distance metric

```python

instance._create_index(dim=len(embeddings[0]), distance_metric="COSINE")
```

The tests would all pass
author: eyurtsev

body:
Got it--By ensuring the expected results for `k=2` are unique to each metric, then we can guard against someone accidentally re-hardcoding the distance metric somewhere.

Will implement.
author: EandrewJones

body:
@eyurtsev I've updated the test to include assertions unique to each distance metric by leveraging the scores produced for the second document when `k=2`.

Simple but it should cover the above concern you raise.
author: EandrewJones

body: out of curiosity, what's value of having a class (instead of standalone test methods like above)
author: dev2049

body:
Hey Davis.  Good question. Initially was reusing some logic across each test method in the class which justified it, but I ended up dropping it. I'm happy to go back to standalone functions if that's your preference.

In general, there are numerous benefits to using classes, however:

1. Logical grouping: Putting related integration tests into a class provides a clear logical grouping of test cases based on functionality or components under test. This makes it easier to understand the purpose of the tests, maintain them, and find specific tests when needed.

2. Code organization: Classes allow you to structure your test code in a more organized way, making it easier to read and maintain. You can define common utility methods or helper functions within the class that can be used by multiple test methods, reducing code duplication.

3. Test fixtures and setup/teardown methods: Pytest allows you to define setup and teardown methods for a class, which will be executed before and after each test method within the class. This can be useful for setting up and cleaning up any shared resources or state needed by multiple tests in the class, such as database connections, test data, or other dependencies.

4. Test parameterization: Pytest supports parameterized test methods within a class, which can reduce code duplication by allowing you to define a single test method and run it with different input parameters. This can be helpful for testing different scenarios or edge cases in your integration tests.

5. Test discovery and execution: By organizing tests into classes, you can more easily control test discovery and execution. You can run an entire class of tests or only specific test methods within a class, providing greater flexibility when running your tests.
author: EandrewJones

body:
thanks for explanation @EandrewJones! 

1 + 2 + 5. i think ideally our code is modular enough that any particular test file already is only testing a pretty small set of functionality, that doesn't require much more grouping / organization on top of it. (you can already run only the tests in a file or only a single test function)
3. true, tho hopefully most of our code is simple / modular enough that we can test isolated things pretty easily with out needing too many shared resoruces. im sure it's sometime unavoidable though
4. think you can do this with standalone pretty readily, no? (pytest.fixture and pytest.mark.parametrize and such?)

convention so far has been to stick to standalone functions unless there's clear need for a class so mild preference for that here, but don't feel strongly about it
author: dev2049

body:
I'll go back to standalone. Convention is important until there's an overwhelming case for why we should change it. Eventually, I think the codebase's complexity will grow to a point where more within-file grouping is necessary to minimize repetition and improve organization.

Two other pieces of food for thought:

1. Test surface area grows faster than the feature space.
2. Targeted tests (re: 1+2+5 above) 

For example, all I do in this feature is add a single parameter to a class method but it results in _at least_ 3 test cases. So now there's a 1:3 ratio from feature to tests. With a class, I maintain a 1-1 mapping. Personally, I like 1-1 logical mappings. The src code files and the tests files also adhere to a 1-1 mapping right now. 

As far targeted runs, the one other nice thing I forgot to mention is I can then scope my tests to run just one class within a file.
author: EandrewJones

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

body:
> Why remove the retriever from the title?

There are 3 problems with **retriever** in titles regarding the navigation bar (left bar):
- some titles have it, and some do not. The inconsistent nav looks not good.
- **retriever** is redundant because all items are inside the **Retrievers** list 
- repeating the same word over and over harms fast screening of the navbar
I agree that the pages can be better with "retriever" in the page title, but the nav bar is more important. It is one of the significant problems for now, the overcomplicated nav bar.

body:
Wow! Thank you for getting all the edge cases mapped out and added to the unit tests!

Do you think there will be any issues to re-write this to use python built-ins URL parsing functions instead to extract the video ID instead of relying on regular expressions?

It'll make it easier for other people to update the logic?

Here's an incomplete sketch of what I'm thinking about: 

```python
from urllib.parse import urlparse, parse_qs

test_cases = [ 
    ("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-wtIMTCHWuI", "-wtIMTCHWuI"),
    ("http://youtube.com/watch?v=-wtIMTCHWuI", "-wtIMTCHWuI"),
    ("http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=-wtIMTCHWuI", "-wtIMTCHWuI"),
    ("http://youtu.be/-wtIMTCHWuI", "-wtIMTCHWuI"),
    ("https://youtu.be/-wtIMTCHWuI", "-wtIMTCHWuI"),
    ("https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lalOy8Mbfdc", "lalOy8Mbfdc"),
    ("https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=lalOy8Mbfdc", "lalOy8Mbfdc"),
    ("https://youtube.com/watch?v=lalOy8Mbfdc", "lalOy8Mbfdc"),
    ("http://youtu.be/lalOy8Mbfdc?t=1", "lalOy8Mbfdc"),
    ("http://youtu.be/lalOy8Mbfdc?t=1s", "lalOy8Mbfdc"),
    ("https://youtu.be/lalOy8Mbfdc?t=1", "lalOy8Mbfdc"),
    ("http://www.youtube-nocookie.com/embed/lalOy8Mbfdc?rel=0", "lalOy8Mbfdc"),
    ("https://youtu.be/lalOy8Mbfdc?t=1s", "lalOy8Mbfdc")
]

allowed_schemas = {'http', 'https'}
allowed_netlock = {'youtu.be', 'm.youtube.com', 'youtube.com', 'www.youtube.com', 'www.youtube-nocookie.com', 'youtu.be'}

def parse_video_id(url):
    parsed_url = urlparse(url)
    
    if parsed_url.scheme not in allowed_schemas:
        return None
        
    if parsed_url.netloc not in allowed_netlock:
        return None
    
    if parsed_url.path.endswith('/watch'):
        parsed_query = parse_qs(parsed_url.query)
        if 'v' in parsed_query:
            ids = parsed_query['v']
            return ids if isinstance(ids, str) else ids[0]
    else:
        # Not implemented yet. 
        return url    

for url, result in test_cases:
    video_id = parse_video_id(url)
    print(url)
    print(result)
    print(video_id)
```
author: eyurtsev

Could we use a ValueError here with an informative message? `asserts` raise assertions which as a rule of thumb should not be excepted by calling code and allow the program to crash, ValueErrors on the other hand can be be excepted by calling code.

Apologies in advance if this suggestion breaks the code, hard to get the whitespaces specified correctly through the UI.

```suggestion
        if not match:
                raise ValueError(f"Could not determine the video ID for the URL {youtube_url}")
```
author: eyurtsev

body:
> Thank you for getting all the edge cases mapped out and added to the unit tests!

My list is not exhaustive but should be enough for majority of LangChain users. I got these from: https://gist.github.com/rodrigoborgesdeoliveira/987683cfbfcc8d800192da1e73adc486

> Do you think there will be any issues to re-write this to use python

No, it's not a problem. I can do it.

Depends on how wide range of cases do we want to support.

Before final decision please consider:

**This regex is mature solution and handles any YT url LangChain users will probably ever encounter. `urllib.parse` is more readable and maintainable but it'd be difficult to reach the scope regex covers.**

It's stripped down version based on much more elaborate one from Youtube-dl source: https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/blob/211cbfd5d46025a8e4d8f9f3d424aaada4698974/youtube_dl/extractor/youtube.py#L524

I've used it as i believe yt-dl has one of the most battle tested extractors available. I doubt it will have to be updated beyond what's in PR.

But i fully agree with readability and maintainability concern. 

What do you think?
author: KaczuH

body:
I agree that the referenced source is battle tested, so it's likely handling all the variations seen in the wild. Do you know if there are accompanying unit tests for the source implementation that we could use in the form of a sequence of URLs? 

Why did you need to strip down the original implementation? Given that the implementation is different from the source, it's hard for me to tell at a glance whether an issue has been introduced.

The other concern with regexps that have such complexity is that they may be prone to regexps attacks.

@vowelparrot   / @dev2049 could you weigh in here with any thoughts?
author: eyurtsev

body:
> Do you know if there are accompanying unit tests for the source implementation that we could use in the form of a sequence of URLs?

There is only one test i've found but it does not test all cases.
https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/blob/211cbfd5d46025a8e4d8f9f3d424aaada4698974/test/test_youtube_misc.py#L14

`YoutubeIE._TESTS` class variable seems to have more urls.
https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/blob/211cbfd5d46025a8e4d8f9f3d424aaada4698974/youtube_dl/extractor/youtube.py#L570

I've already gone through them. All cases are in the PR.

> Why did you need to strip down the original implementation?

The original regex is too excessive. It handles any known address redirecting to YT videos (youtuberepeat, various video downloaders, alternative frontends like Invidious etc). I assumed Langchain users don't need that. What i'd expect from `YoutubeLoader.from_youtube_url` (btw this name should be less verbose e.g.`YoutubeLoader.from_url`) to correctly handle most of standard urls.

 > The other concern with regexps that have such complexity is that they may be prone to regexps attacks.
 
I agree.

I'm not gonna push it. I just want to provide as much context as possible to facilitate decision making process.
author: KaczuH

body:
Hello, how about calling the first item of the dictionary? @mcleonard @tmynn @dev2049 @agola11 
```
aim.Text(list(inputs_res.values())[0]), name="on_chain_start", context=resp
```

For `search` chain, **inputs_res** has 'input' key.  However, for `math` chain, **inputs_res** has only 'question' key, as you find below. 
```
> Entering new  chain...
{'input': "Who is Leo DiCaprio's girlfriend? What is her current age raised to the 0.43 power?"}
 I need to find out who Leo DiCaprio's girlfriend is and then calculate her age raised to the 0.43 power.
Action: Search
Action Input: "Leo DiCaprio girlfriend"
Observation: The Oscar-winner was then linked to Gigi Hadid, 28, in September 2022.
Thought: I need to calculate Gigi Hadid's age raised to the 0.43 power.
Action: Calculator
Action Input: 28^0.43

> Entering new  chain...
{'question': '28^0.43'}
Error in on_chain_start callback: 'input'
28^0.43
\```text
28**0.43
\```
...numexpr.evaluate("28**0.43")...

Answer: 4.1906168361987195
> Finished chain.
Error in on_chain_end callback: 'output'

Observation: Answer: 4.1906168361987195
Thought: I now know the final answer.
Final Answer: Gigi Hadid, 28, is Leo DiCaprio's girlfriend and her age raised to the 0.43 power is 4.1906168361987195.

> Finished chain.
```

Same solution goes for `on_chain_end`.
Looking forward fixing these trivial errors.

body:
`load` should not take arguments or kwargs, so the interface remains `load()`

```suggestion
    def load(self) -> List[Document]:
```

All configuration differences get restricted to the __init__,  making it possible to substitute one loader for another in code that relies on loaders having a uniform load interface.

body:
Hello! Thanks for the contribution! 

These abstract methods are much better as a single standalone function that takes in a file path and returns additional metadata. 

We could perhaps add a single abstractmethod that loads this standardized metadata from the function.

This function can then be invoked as appropriate by whatever loader.

author: eyurtsev

body:
Let's avoid using unstructured for file type detection as metadata should be handled consistently across document loaders.

One option would be to standardize around: `mimetype`  and `suffix` (file suffix capturing the substring after the last `.` including the `.`)
author: eyurtsev

body: Thanks for the advice given! Updated accordingly.
author: marcusyatim

body:
Thanks for the advice given! I agree that it will be tricky designing it but I look forward to the challenge and hopefully can help you standardize metadata handling across the various loaders :)

I have taken the liberty of creating a script for this in a new folder `./langchain/document_loader/utils/`, called `metadata.py`. Basically, I wanted to abstract the metadata retrieval from the main document loader scripts and put them all together into `metadata.py`. Inside, we can have helper classes for each document loader to do the metadata retrieval.

For example, we can have the class `UnstructuredMetadata()` and it takes in the file path (as you have suggested) (or alternatively, file IO, if necessary), and its class methods will get metadata like "source", "create_at", "update_at", "mime_type" and "extension".
author: marcusyatim

body: Thanks for the advice given! Updated accordingly.
author: marcusyatim

body:
Agreed on that. I have taken the liberty of creating a script for this in a new folder `./langchain/document_loader/utils/`, called `filetype.py`. It contains helper functions that can retrieve (at the moment) a couple of file type related info: mime type and file extension. 

Could even add in more helper functions in the future for any file type related info.
author: marcusyatim

body:
Wanted to give the option to decide what metadata to be returned. For whatever unique use cases there may be. 

Default is return all metadata.
author: marcusyatim

body: Leaving these here as abstract methods because we still don't know what are all the potential methods we have in mind. Future contributors can also refer here for implementation guide or even ideas for new methods.
author: marcusyatim

body:
Anything related to metadata retrieval and can placed here. And is a good place to help standardize across the various document loaders.

One thing I have to say about this script is that if we were to include support for all the document loaders, e.g.  twitter, reddit, pdf, url, unstructured, etc., this will be a very very long script. Not too sure what are the appropriate design guidelines here.

Or would it be better to just have the metadata retrieval inside the individual document loader scripts?
author: marcusyatim

With this change, https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/23083e6a5c555e7c274b91658251edfd2eed0a82/docs/modules/models/llms/integrations/gpt4all.ipynb?short_path=20a420a#L130 can be simplified to

llm = GPT4All(model="ggml-gpt4all-j-v1.3-groovy", callbacks=callbacks, verbose=True)
```
similar to the official instruction, https://github.com/nomic-ai/gpt4all/blob/0f71cb6759f648ad210d2b4bdd56d5b3e94b8d37/gpt4all-bindings/python/README.md?plain=1#L41

I'd not rely on auto-suggested backend cause it's simply [hardcoded](https://github.com/nomic-ai/gpt4all/blob/0f71cb6759f648ad210d2b4bdd56d5b3e94b8d37/gpt4all-bindings/python/gpt4all/gpt4all.py#L274) as well as the [default model path](https://github.com/nomic-ai/gpt4all/blob/0f71cb6759f648ad210d2b4bdd56d5b3e94b8d37/gpt4all-bindings/python/gpt4all/gpt4all.py#L15). someone may rename their model.bin
idk why there is some logic around it ;)
may be it's better to add `model_path=model_path or './'` because linux users might expect the path to be relative as the default behavior for shell/bash/etc.

body: I'm not suggesting to rely on auto-suggested backend; even with this change, user can still use the old way, i.e., providing specific installation path rather than auto-suggested one by providing the local path (which includes the `/` part, so the `model_path` will not be falsy). What I suggested is to provide an option to choose auto-suggested backend if the user wanted to use it.

Even if we try to look up the models against the API I think we still run into the following issue:

Since the foundational models and the tuned models are separate resources, publisher model vs registered model, I don' think we can assume the resource IDs will globally unique leading to ambiguous intention if the resource ID is reused across resources. 

It may be safer to expose these as separate parameters. It may also evolve better with the Vertex SDK since it aligns with it's semantics.

body:
query = query or self.query allows passing the same information from 2 different places (init and the method)

This pattern tends to introduce design and usage issues as the code grows in complexity

e.g., for usage a user now has to know which query has precedence
author: eyurtsev

body:
The current loader restricts configuration to be provided in the  __ init __, to produce a fully curried `load` method.

The goal is to introduce the most minimal interface possible to go from raw bytes stored somewhere to documents that can be indexed. (We may even remove the `load_and_split` functionality in the future (when we're ready to commit to that breaking change)).

My thought is to try and create the smallest re-usable building blocks so we can lean heavily on compositional patterns and allow users to combine the building blocks in whichever way they want.
author: eyurtsev

body:
This shows one way (which I don't love) to deal with the fact that config information must be supplied at initialization https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/4731/files

Anyway we may revisit this since I agree that being unable to provide config through the method leads to some akward downstream patterns
author: eyurtsev

body:
Yes, I agree. Still, the Class initiation parameters naturally have lower precedence in regard to the calling parameters.
I'd rather completely move `query` from `__init__`. But it will be a breaking change.

To make sure that the space in which vector store is initiated is reduced to a user scope. Something like` filter={'user_id': {'$eq': self.user_id}},`
Then any other filtering might happen afterward.
I am imagining a case where for each user there are thousands of records, so pre-filtering for a user might speed up the operations, vs combining it with other filters and repeating the same condition down the line.

body:
It's in the `BaseCallbackHandler` via the `CallbackManagerMixin` as a not implemented error so that existing callbacks' behavior won't be impacted.

It should either be fully implemented or ignored. If it throws a `NotImplementedError`, we use the `on_llm_start` handler as a fallback. Overwriting with a pass means the event will be silently handled and won't be printed.


Since this is a console handler, I'd just remove this function and let it fallback to the `on_llm_start` method unless we want to have different formatting than the "`get_buffer_string`" implementation

We could also replace the throw error / catch with a class variable on whether it's implemented or do something fancier in the class init to infer that if we don't like it.

if api_token is None and context is not None:
