body:
@dev2049 The logic here is by design. It will look up the api_token in following logic:
* method parameter `api_token`
* ENV variable `DATABRICKS_API_TOKEN`
* If in Databricks notebook, there will be a temp token from `context.apiToken`
* If api_token is still None, throw error since it is required. The method `utils.get_from_env` can throw a proper exception.

body:
in MRKL agent, the prompt is formatted using format, e.g. here it would be :
`self.trial_prefix.format(trial_number = str(trial_number))`

From my understanding, the difference with this alternative is that it would raise an error if {trial_number} is not present in self.trial_prefix (while the "replace" implementation would just silently do nothing). Depending on the behavior that you want in this edge case, you may stick to the current implementation (with replace) or the one I provided here (with format) ;-)

body: Optionals are values that can take on a None value. Except for `loader_kwargs` (and file patterns) no parameters should be allowed to take a None value.
author: eyurtsev

body:
:wave:  @marcusyatim 

The `file_pattern` named argument should be pushed to the end of the list of arguments to avoid breaking changes. 

The code base isn't relying on the `*` separator in function signatures, so users may be invoking this initializer with parameters passed by position rather than name.

The argument name should be pluralized to follow standard naming conventions (`file_patterns`)

```
file_patterns: Optional[Sequence[str]] = None
```
author: eyurtsev

body:
A generic `file_patterns` can be pretty confusing in terms of what it does. The thing I'd propose we do here is a filter by suffix that includes the `.` as part of the filename.

The glob continues to work as always, followed by a potential filter that ignores files that don't match the requested set of suffixes.
author: eyurtsev

body: How many times is p.rglob executed per file pattern? There should be a single invocation of the glob pattern irrespective of the number of file patterns, followed by a filter that checks that the file matches the selection criteria
author: eyurtsev

body: `self.file_pattern` performs a mutation of class state outside of `__init__` -- this is not a safe programming pattern, so we avoid it in the langchain code base as much as possible.

body: will let you decide on the best interface here, but we've found it's often nice to pass instance variable in directly to constructor (in this case just the logger) and then to have a convenience classmethod called something like `from_params` that takes in all the individual params and assembles logger and passes it in. that way you get both a convenient constructor if you want, and if somebody wants to fully control how logger is constructed and pass that in themselves, they can do that, too. also makes things a bit more robust to future changes e.g. if logger interface changes, from_params will be out-of-sync but user can still construct logger themselves and pass it in

body: great catch! i wonder if we should just change list(doc) -> [doc] and leave the rest the same. reason being current implementation lets us lazily load in texts, whereas calling list(texts) up front would load all of them into memory
author: dev2049

body:
> great catch! i wonder if we should just change list(doc) -> [doc] and leave the rest the same. reason being current implementation lets us lazily load in texts, whereas calling list(texts) up front would load all of them into memory

I checked the `add_texts` methods of all vectore stores. Here is what I found:

1. **Twelve** vector stores turn the potential iterable `texts` into a list first and then embed them:
* chroma
* pgvector
* qdrant
* supabase
* analyticdb
* atlas
* deeplake
* elastic_vector_search
* lancedb
* milvus
* opensearch_vector_search
* tair

2. **Four** vector stores that iterate over `texts` and embed text one by one lazily:
* faiss
* redis
* pinecone
* myscale

The behavious are not consistent. But most vector stores simply turn the `texts` variable into a list first.
author: Shawn91

body: yea we definitely haven't been consistent about it, which is on us. and it's possible for a lot of workloads / embedding methods that embedding all the texts at once is more efficient. but think i'd prefer to not change behavior in an existing vector store until we've come up with a best practice that we apply everywhere. meaning in this case slight preference to keep lazy for now (otherwise somebody who's using Weaviate today could see their memory usage change next update for seemingly no reason)

       if sys.platform.startswith("linux") or sys.platform.startswith("win"):
         tools.append(AzureCogsImageAnalysisTool())
       return tools
```

Something along these lines for the file:

```python

from __future__ import annotations

import pytest

from langchain.agents.conversational_chat.output_parser import ConvoOutputParser, OutputParserException


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "text, expected",
    [
        ('text', (expected value) ),

    ]
)
def test_parser(text, expected) -> None:
    """Test the ChatOutputParser class."""
    parser = ConvoOutputParser()
    assert parser.parse(text) == expected
    
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "text",
    [
        ('texts that lead to parse exception'),

    ]
)
def test_unhappy_path(text) -> None:
    """Test the ChatOutputParser class."""
    parser = ConvoOutputParser()
    with pytest.raises(OutputParserException):
        parser.parse(text)
```

body:
Could you provide a benchmark for this?

Since list comprehensions are fast and this code involves mutating lists, I don't know if it's obvious what version will be fater:

```python
a = [ (x, x+1) for x in range(int(1e7)) ]

x = []
y = []

for z in a:
    x.append(z[0])
    y.append(z[1])
```

```python
a = [ (x, x+1) for x in range(int(1e7)) ]

x = [ z[0] for z in a ]
y = [ z[1] for z in a ]
```
author: eyurtsev

body: @eyurtsev See below for a benchmark analysis across different document lengths.
author: startakovsky

body:
yes sorry, you can initialize the model indeed.
I meant when you generate a response from your initialized OpenAI model.
You always call `get_sub_prompts` which in turns calls `max_tokens_for_prompt` that finally calls `modelname_to_contextsize`. However, `max_tokens_for_prompt` is only called if `params["max_tokens"] == -1`.
Ok. Then yes, I agree, most general solution is to create a helper function.
author: tommasodelorenzo

body:
What about something like

```
def get_based_model_name(
    model_name:str,
) -> str:
    if "ft-" in model_name:
        model_name = f"{model_name.split(':')[0]}-finetuned"

    return model_name

def get_openai_token_cost_for_model(
    model_name: str, num_tokens: int, is_completion: bool = False
) -> float:

    model_name = get_based_model_name(model_name)

    suffix = "-completion" if is_completion and model_name.startswith("gpt-4") else ""
    model = model_name.lower() + suffix
    if model not in MODEL_COST_PER_1K_TOKENS:
        raise ValueError(
            f"Unknown model: {model_name}. Please provide a valid OpenAI model name."
            "Known models are: " + ", ".join(MODEL_COST_PER_1K_TOKENS.keys())
        )
    return MODEL_COST_PER_1K_TOKENS[model] * num_tokens / 1000

...

def on_llm_end(self, response: LLMResult, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        """Collect token usage."""
        if response.llm_output is None:
            return None
        self.successful_requests += 1
        if "token_usage" not in response.llm_output:
            return None
        token_usage = response.llm_output["token_usage"]
        completion_tokens = token_usage.get("completion_tokens", 0)
        prompt_tokens = token_usage.get("prompt_tokens", 0)
        model_name = get_based_model_name(
            response.llm_output.get("model_name")
        )
        if model_name and model_name in MODEL_COST_PER_1K_TOKENS:
            completion_cost = get_openai_token_cost_for_model(
                model_name, completion_tokens, is_completion=True
            )
            prompt_cost = get_openai_token_cost_for_model(model_name, prompt_tokens)
            self.total_cost += prompt_cost + completion_cost
        self.total_tokens += token_usage.get("total_tokens", 0)
        self.prompt_tokens += prompt_tokens
        self.completion_tokens += completion_tokens
```

body:
Yes this is pagination. Following is an example how to use it. I have also added this in the docs in my latest commit.

```python
query = """
Set.paginate("hs+DzoPOg ... aY1hOohozrV7A")
"""
loader = FaunaLoader(query, field, secret)
```

You get a `after` value if there are more data. You can get values after the curcor by passing in the `after` string in query. 

To learn more following [this link](https://fqlx-beta--fauna-docs.netlify.app/fqlx/beta/reference/schema_entities/set/static-paginate)
author: Shadid12

body:

body:
@ByronHsu @dev2049 

Instead of creating new classes for each language, could we parameterize by language? 

It'll save at least for lines of code per new language that's introduced, and also make it easier to see what languages are available.

```python

from typing import Union, Literal

Language = Union[Literal['go'], Literal['python'], Literal[...]]

class CodeTextSplitter(RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter):
    def __init__(self, language: Language, **kwargs: Any):
        separators = get_separators_for_language(language)
        super().__init__(separators=separators, **kwargs)
```

```

body:
This looks good to me. 

Have you considered using a union of literals instead of an enum?

```
Language = Union[Literal['go'], Literal['js'], ...]
```

Gives the same static typing guarantees and makes it such that a user does not have to import an extra enum.

I think the only downside is that it's harder to list all the options in the set.

In [4]: list(A)
Out[4]: [<A.foo: 'foo'>, <A.bar: 'bar'>]

body:
> Would it make sense to expose this as a public method that the user can invoke if they need it?

No. Given the way the class has been designed, schema creation has to happen before they upload docs into the database. I'd rather make it implicit than count on the user to make the call themselves.
author: hsm207

body:
> Should any of the schema parameters be exposed? e.g., the vectorizer?

Given the way the class is designed, I think the only parameter that should be exposed is the vectorizer. However, we could also use langchain to the vectorization instead of assuming that the user has set up weaviate with the vectorizer module enabled. Which approach would you like to go support?
author: hsm207

body: I generally think that good defaults with flexibility is better for users. But I don't have insight into how users are using this, so no strong opinions. :)
author: eyurtsev

body:
The usage we've seen in other frameworks are that they will use other the framework's embeddings feature to generate the embeddings. The vector db is meant to just store and retrieve the embeddings.

Let's keep this like this for now and reconsider again based on user feedback. E.g. if a user wants to use GPT4All for the embeddings then we definitely need to rewrite this class.
author: hsm207

body:
What do you think about adding a parameter that allows a user to control this behavior in the init?

The reason I'm concerned is that this pattern make a network request on every initialization of the retriever (checking if schema exists). This will get in the way of any users trying to move this retriever into production. The user's code may involve single instantiation of the weaviate client, but many instantiations of the retriever with the assumption that there isn't any over-head.
author: eyurtsev

body:
> What do you think about adding a parameter that allows a user to control this behavior in the init?

This is a good idea if the user already has an identical schema inside weaviate. If not, a schema has to be created anyway because we need to tell weaviate which vectorizer to use.

so, assuming the param is called create_schema, is this how you'd see the user's code could look like?

```python
x = WeaviateHybridSearchRetriever(client, index_name="Foo", text_key="text")

body: Just wanted to provide the following information: in MongoDB, a namespace (database + collection - like a table in SQL) can have several indexes. By removing the parameters allowing the user to change the default namespace and index, it means we are making a one to one mapping between a tuple of (namespace, index) and a vectorstore instance (MongoDBAtlasVectorSearch). So if the user wants to perform a similarity search in a different namespace and index, they will have to create a new instance.
author: P-E-B

This is the primary reason why I used the client: MongoClient parameter instead so it could be passed in.

@dev2049 @P-E-B I am working on a vector search support for the MongoDB API for CosmosDB and would like to add this support in this client, if possible. Could we abstract the implementation so that it could be adapted to support multiple flavors of MongoDB server types (Atlas, CosmosDB, upstream open source Mongo)?

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/mongodb/vcore/vector-search#create-a-vectorindex

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/mongodb/vcore/vector-search#performing-a-vector-search

The search syntax is slightly different for each server type but majority of the logic would be similar.

Would it be ok to include support for CosmosDB Mongo here by making a few adaptations to make it compatible with multiple Mongo server types or should we make that a separate vector story type?

I would be waiting for your feedback on how to proceed.

body:
There is only one type of MongoDB server : it's the mongod process.
The vector search is not available in MongoDB. Only in MongoDB Atlas that abstracts Lucene behind the scene. Each vendor should introduce its own vectorstore since the underlying technologies can be very different and that it has been the current implementation. There is already a MongoDB Atlas vectorstore in the JS langchain library. IMO let's keep it that way.
author: P-E-B

when `metadata_str` is the metadata converted to a string.

@ryderwishart  thank you for the contribution! If you're interested in working on this a few guidelines: 

1. We don't want to use `print` statements in the code -- i'm seeing some other print statements here, but i think verbosity should be controlled with the callbacks probably

4. If I'm not mistaken these logical filters can be nested, so the fix may need to be applied through the filter tree

body:
So, the actual generation of the structured query happens at `langchain/chains/query_constructor/base.py`:
```python
def load_query_constructor_chain(
    llm: BaseLanguageModel,
    document_contents: str,
    attribute_info: List[AttributeInfo],
    examples: Optional[List] = None,
    allowed_comparators: Optional[Sequence[Comparator]] = None,
    allowed_operators: Optional[Sequence[Operator]] = None,
    **kwargs: Any,
) -> LLMChain:
    prompt = _get_prompt(
        document_contents, # NOTE: Input goes here...
        attribute_info, # NOTE: Schema for database metadata
        examples=examples,
        allowed_comparators=allowed_comparators, # NOTE: equals, greater than, less than, etc.
        allowed_operators=allowed_operators, # NOTE: and, or, not, potentially extensible list
    )
    print('prompt: ', prompt)
    return LLMChain(llm=llm, prompt=prompt, **kwargs)
```

This output (the LLM-generated structured query) gets passed to
```python
class SelfQueryRetriever(BaseRetriever, BaseModel):
    # [...]
    @classmethod
        def from_llm(
            cls,
            llm: BaseLanguageModel,
            vectorstore: VectorStore,
            document_contents: str,
            metadata_field_info: List[AttributeInfo],
            structured_query_translator: Optional[Visitor] = None,
            chain_kwargs: Optional[Dict] = None,
            enable_limit: bool = False,
            **kwargs: Any,
        ) -> "SelfQueryRetriever":
            if structured_query_translator is None: # NOTE: this translator is vectorstore-specific
                structured_query_translator = _get_builtin_translator(vectorstore.__class__)
            chain_kwargs = chain_kwargs or {}
    
            if "allowed_comparators" not in chain_kwargs:
                chain_kwargs[
                    "allowed_comparators"
                ] = structured_query_translator.allowed_comparators
            if "allowed_operators" not in chain_kwargs:
                chain_kwargs[
                    "allowed_operators"
                ] = structured_query_translator.allowed_operators
            llm_chain = load_query_constructor_chain( # NOTE: Here is where we call load_query_constructor_chain
                llm,
                document_contents,
                metadata_field_info,
                enable_limit=enable_limit,
                **chain_kwargs,
            )
            return cls(
                llm_chain=llm_chain, # NOTE: here is where we use the predicted structured query
                vectorstore=vectorstore,
                structured_query_translator=structured_query_translator, # NOTE: the predicted structured query gets parsed in a vectorstore-specific manner
                **kwargs,
            )
```

My first attempt to fix this upstream, accordingly, was to adjust the prompt that is actually called by `load_query_constructor_chain`, to try to direct the LLM to respect compound operators such as 'AND' and 'OR'. See [below](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/5570#discussion_r1213585025). 

Since this did not achieve the desired effect, I'm still left wondering if there actually is a better place to fix this issue upstream. Perhaps some vectorstores can gracefully handle compound operators with only one argument (e.g., 'OR [sole condition]'), but that is unexpected behaviour, in my opinion. 

Another spot that might make sense is `visit_structured_query`, in this function:

```python
    def get_relevant_documents(self, query: str) -> List[Document]:
        """Get documents relevant for a query.

        Args:
            query: string to find relevant documents for

        Returns:
            List of relevant documents
        """
        inputs = self.llm_chain.prep_inputs({"query": query})
        structured_query = cast(
            StructuredQuery, self.llm_chain.predict_and_parse(callbacks=None, **inputs)
        )
        if self.verbose:
            print(structured_query)

        new_query, new_kwargs = self.structured_query_translator.visit_structured_query(
            structured_query
        )
        if structured_query.limit is not None:
            new_kwargs["k"] = structured_query.limit

        search_kwargs = {**self.search_kwargs, **new_kwargs}
        docs = self.vectorstore.search(new_query, self.search_type, **search_kwargs)
        return docs
```

But that function is abstract, and its implementation is defined relative to the different vectorstores. I would hope to not only fix this problem for Chroma, for example, but all types of vectorstores, so this seems like the wrong place to fix it, as well.

The output parser, in turn, unpacks the JSON response from the LLM output. This might be a logical place to process the actual content of the response, but I suspect it makes more sense to let that module handle extracting the query and filter JSON only.

Anyways, what I'm left with at this point is actually my original implementation, in which `SelfQueryRetriever`'s `get_relevant_documents` method recognizes that it cannot use compound operators with one argument. 

I think this fits here given the current implementation, because `get_relevant_documents` actually manipulates the arguments already:

```python
new_query, new_kwargs = self.structured_query_translator.visit_structured_query(
    structured_query 
) # NOTE: can't handle the compound operators here, because visit_structured_query is abstract
if structured_query.limit is not None:
    new_kwargs["k"] = structured_query.limit # NOTE: direct manipulation of arguments already taking place

search_kwargs = {**self.search_kwargs, **new_kwargs}
docs = self.vectorstore.search(new_query, self.search_type, **search_kwargs)
return docs
```

Anyways, that's the result of my digging: back where I started. I think the alternative is to update the StructureQuery/FilterDirective types to recognize two types of operators, compound (AND/OR) and unary (NOT). That would mean updating all of the prompt templates, visitor functions, and structured query parsers for all of the implementations, since the functions are abstract. Happy to look elsewhere though if you have other suggestions!

If not, it would be nice to just work with the current PR for now so I don't have to worry about manually catching the problem in my implementation. We could always open an issue for a more thorough follow up.
author: ryderwishart

body:
One other idea I have is to try modifying the prompt template to include negative examples as well, which would include something like `and(eq({'artist': 'Taylor Swift'}))` with an explanation of what's wrong. However, my concern is that if you switch LLMs another model might not catch on easily, even if I could get it working with OpenAI. 

Manually setting the 'k' value and dropping unnecessary wrappers together at filter implementation seems like a more resilient solution.

body:
Minor nit on this: the `bytes` object is created by calling `f.read()` inside a with statement doesn't have a name attribute associated with it, which throws an error w/ the Whisper API. 

```
packages/openai/api_resources/audio.py:55, in Audio.transcribe(cls, model, file, api_key, api_base, api_type, api_version, organization, **params)
     43 @classmethod
     44 def transcribe(
     45     cls,
   (...)
     53     **params,
     54 ):
---> 55     requestor, files, data = cls._prepare_request(file, file.name, model, **params)
     56     url = cls._get_url("transcriptions")
     57     response, _, api_key = requestor.request("post", url, files=files, params=data)

AttributeError: 'bytes' object has no attribute 'name'
```

We may work around it with something like:
```
with open(self.audio_file_path, 'rb') as f:
    audio_data = f.read()
audio_file = io.BytesIO(audio_data)
audio_file.name = self.audio_file_path
```

body:
The role of the parser is to make sure that we can implement parsing/transformation logic on raw bytes without caring about where the raw bytes came from (they can be in memory or on disk or on s3 or on a website).

```python
import openai
from langchain.document_loaders.generic import GenericLoader

class OpenAIWhisperParser(BaseBlobParser):
    """Transcribe and parse  audio files using audio-to-text transcription with OpenAI Whisper model."""

    def lazy_parse(self, blob: Blob) -> Iterator[Document]:
        """Lazily parse the blob."""
        import openai
        
        with blob.as_bytes_io() as f:
            transcript = openai.Audio.transcribe('whisper-1', f)
        yield Document(page_content=transcript.text,metadata={"source": blob.source})

loader = GenericLoader.from_filesystem('directory', glob="*.mp3", parser=OpenAIWhisperParser())
docs = loader.load()
```

In principle, we don't even need to introduce a new loader since the GenericLoader can handle this.

One inconvenience is that at the moment it only supports loading patterns from the file system, so we can augment it to pick up a blob from a specific filepath 

`GenericLoader.from_path()` or something of this sort

We should figure out how to organize the files. 

Here, we're dealing with classes that are called blob transformers which transform binary data to binary data of other format.

These are very similar to parsers. Parsers transform blobs to documents. Documents can be viewed as a blob that's been loaded into memory and looks like textual data.

I don't have a strong opinion at the moment, but I think we should think for a bit about the name spacing to make the code easy to discover (and relatively fast to import!)

OK this is a good work around the fact that yt_dlp doesn't allow downloading the bytes directly into memory. 

----

One thing to note is the work is organized as follows:
(1) first download all the audio, and only then 
(2) yield audio blobs one at a time

However, in other cases the work is organized as: yield blobs one at a time (as they're available). 

If we know what title and ext are (from the `info`), we can achieve the latter work organization by instantiating the blob from within the loop directly with the correct path.

---

Only pointing this out, but it's not important at the moment how we organize the work, so we can use the current version
author: eyurtsev

Let's create a buffered reader here
```
from io import BytesIO, BufferedReader
b = io.BytesIO(b'some data')
b.name = "hello.mp3"
b.seek(0)
reader = BufferedReader(b)
```

We can then remove the context manager that uses `blob.as_bytes_io()`, so we avoid reading the blob data twice.

---

We can follow up later and add this capability to the blob object to allow it to yield a named buffered reader.

body: The goal of this PR is to offer better security in use of Python REPL, and passing in `globals()` looks like a massive security hole to me. I see a lot of code out there where people expose e.g. their API keys and other secrets as globals in memory space, and those could be a prompt injection target. The rest of your comments, @hwchase17 seem to be towards making a higher security level optional, and I'd reluctantly accept that (for me, higher security should be the default), but if the user does opt for a tighter ship, then that `_globals=None` is IMO an important part of the picture.
author: uogbuji

body: agreed with @uogbuji but also right now the Wasm interpreter doesn't supported globals that can't be represented as a type that can be easily converted to string. Often the result of the globals function call are object types that can't be converted to string.

In the updated code, the reset_cache_state fixture is defined with the autouse=True parameter, which ensures that it is automatically used by all test functions. The fixture resets the langchain.llm_cache to None before each test, and after each test, ensuring a clean cache state for each test function.

By using the reset_cache_state fixture, the cache state will be reset for each test, providing test isolation.

body:
sync is an overloaded term, is it 1-way sync or 2-way sync, is it mirror-like (ie. it deletes everything in destination that isn't in source, etc). To give you an idea the design space here see below as example help page for `gsutil rsync`
Details

NAME rsync - Synchronize content of two buckets/directories SYNOPSIS gsutil rsync [OPTION]... src_url dst_url DESCRIPTION The gsutil rsync command makes the contents under dst_url the same as the contents under src_url, by copying any missing files/objects (or those whose data has changed), and (if the -d option is specified) deleting any extra files/objects. src_url must specify a directory, bucket, or bucket subdirectory. For example, to sync the contents of the local directory "data" to the bucket gs://mybucket/data, you could do: gsutil rsync data gs://mybucket/data To recurse into directories use the -r option: gsutil rsync -r data gs://mybucket/data If you have a large number of objects to synchronize you might want to use the gsutil -m option (see "gsutil help options"), to perform parallel (multi-threaded/multi-processing) synchronization: gsutil -m rsync -r data gs://mybucket/data The -m option typically will provide a large performance boost if either the source or destination (or both) is a cloud URL. If both source and destination are file URLs the -m option will typically thrash the disk and slow synchronization down. Note 1: Shells (like bash, zsh) sometimes attempt to expand wildcards in ways that can be surprising. Also, attempting to copy files whose names contain wildcard characters can result in problems. For more details about these issues see `Wildcard behavior considerations `_. Note 2: If you are synchronizing a large amount of data between clouds you might consider setting up a `Google Compute Engine `_ account and running gsutil there. Since cross-provider gsutil data transfers flow through the machine where gsutil is running, doing this can make your transfer run significantly faster than running gsutil on your local workstation. Note 3: rsync does not copy empty directory trees, since Cloud Storage uses a `flat namespace `_. Using -d Option (with caution!) to mirror source and destination. The rsync -d option is very useful and commonly used, because it provides a means of making the contents of a destination bucket or directory match those of a source bucket or directory. This is done by copying all data from the source to the destination and deleting all other data in the destination that is not in the source. Please exercise caution when you use this option: It's possible to delete large amounts of data accidentally if, for example, you erroneously reverse source and destination. To make the local directory my-data the same as the contents of gs://mybucket/data and delete objects in the local directory that are not in gs://mybucket/data: gsutil rsync -d -r gs://mybucket/data my-data To make the contents of gs://mybucket2 the same as gs://mybucket1 and delete objects in gs://mybucket2 that are not in gs://mybucket1: gsutil rsync -d -r gs://mybucket1 gs://mybucket2 You can also mirror data across local directories. This example will copy all objects from dir1 into dir2 and delete all objects in dir2 which are not in dir1: gsutil rsync -d -r dir1 dir2 To mirror your content across clouds: gsutil rsync -d -r gs://my-gs-bucket s3://my-s3-bucket Change detection works if the other Cloud provider is using md5 or CRC32. AWS multipart upload has an incompatible checksum. As mentioned above, using -d can be dangerous because of how quickly data can be deleted. For example, if you meant to synchronize a local directory from a bucket in the cloud but instead run the command: gsutil -m rsync -r -d ./your-dir gs://your-bucket and your-dir is currently empty, you will quickly delete all of the objects in gs://your-bucket. You can also cause large amounts of data to be lost quickly by specifying a subdirectory of the destination as the source of an rsync. For example, the command: gsutil -m rsync -r -d gs://your-bucket/data gs://your-bucket would cause most or all of the objects in gs://your-bucket to be deleted (some objects may survive if there are any with names that sort lower than "data" under gs://your-bucket/data). In addition to paying careful attention to the source and destination you specify with the rsync command, there are two more safety measures you can take when using gsutil rsync -d: 1. Try running the command with the rsync -n option first, to see what it would do without actually performing the operations. For example, if you run the command: gsutil -m rsync -r -d -n gs://your-bucket/data gs://your-bucket it will be immediately evident that running that command without the -n option would cause many objects to be deleted. 2. Enable object versioning in your bucket, which allows you to restore objects if you accidentally delete them. For more details see `Object Versioning `_. BE CAREFUL WHEN SYNCHRONIZING OVER OS-SPECIFIC FILE TYPES (SYMLINKS, DEVICES, ETC.) Running gsutil rsync over a directory containing operating system-specific file types (symbolic links, device files, sockets, named pipes, etc.) can cause various problems. For example, running a command like: gsutil rsync -r ./dir gs://my-bucket will cause gsutil to follow any symbolic links in ./dir, creating objects in my-bucket containing the data from the files to which the symlinks point. This can cause various problems: * If you use gsutil rsync as a simple way to backup a directory to a bucket, restoring from that bucket will result in files where the symlinks used to be. At best this is wasteful of space, and at worst it can result in outdated data or broken applications -- depending on what is consuming the symlinks. * If you use gsutil rsync over directories containing broken symlinks, gsutil rsync will abort (unless you pass the -e option). * gsutil rsync skips symlinks that point to directories. Since gsutil rsync is intended to support data operations (like moving a data set to the cloud for computational processing) and it needs to be compatible both in the cloud and across common operating systems, there are no plans for gsutil rsync to support operating system-specific file types like symlinks. We recommend that users do one of the following: * Don't use gsutil rsync over directories containing symlinks or other OS- specific file types. * Use the -e option to exclude symlinks or the -x option to exclude OS-specific file types by name. * Use a tool (such as tar) that preserves symlinks and other OS-specific file types, packaging up directories containing such files before uploading to the cloud. EVENTUAL CONSISTENCY WITH NON-GOOGLE CLOUD PROVIDERS While Google Cloud Storage is strongly consistent, some cloud providers only support eventual consistency. You may encounter scenarios where rsync synchronizes using stale listing data when working with these other cloud providers. For example, if you run rsync immediately after uploading an object to an eventually consistent cloud provider, the added object may not yet appear in the provider's listing. Consequently, rsync will miss adding the object to the destination. If this happens you can rerun the rsync operation again later (after the object listing has "caught up"). CHECKSUM VALIDATION AND FAILURE HANDLING At the end of every upload or download, the gsutil rsync command validates that the checksum of the source file/object matches the checksum of the destination file/object. If the checksums do not match, gsutil deletes the invalid copy and print a warning message. This very rarely happens, but if it does, please contact gs-team@google.com. The rsync command retries failures when it is useful to do so, but if enough failures happen during a particular copy or delete operation, or if a failure isn't retryable, the overall command fails. If the -C option is provided, the command instead skips failing objects and moves on. At the end of the synchronization run, if any failures were not successfully retried, the rsync command reports the count of failures and exits with non-zero status. At this point you can run the rsync command again, and gsutil attempts any remaining needed copy and/or delete operations. For more details about gsutil's retry handling, see `Retry strategy `_. CHANGE DETECTION ALGORITHM To determine if a file or object has changed, gsutil rsync first checks whether the file modification time (mtime) of both the source and destination is available. If mtime is available at both source and destination, and the destination mtime is different than the source, or if the source and destination file size differ, gsutil rsync will update the destination. If the source is a cloud bucket and the destination is a local file system, and if mtime is not available for the source, gsutil rsync will use the time created for the cloud object as a substitute for mtime. Otherwise, if mtime is not available for either the source or the destination, gsutil rsync will fall back to using checksums. If the source and destination are both cloud buckets with checksums available, gsutil rsync will use these hashes instead of mtime. However, gsutil rsync will still update mtime at the destination if it is not present. If the source and destination have matching checksums and only the source has an mtime, gsutil rsync will copy the mtime to the destination. If neither mtime nor checksums are available, gsutil rsync will resort to comparing file sizes. Checksums will not be available when comparing composite Google Cloud Storage objects with objects at a cloud provider that does not support CRC32C (which is the only checksum available for composite objects). See 'gsutil help compose' for details about composite objects. COPYING IN THE CLOUD AND METADATA PRESERVATION If both the source and destination URL are cloud URLs from the same provider, gsutil copies data "in the cloud" (i.e., without downloading to and uploading from the machine where you run gsutil). In addition to the performance and cost advantages of doing this, copying in the cloud preserves metadata (like Content-Type and Cache-Control). In contrast, when you download data from the cloud it ends up in a file, which has no associated metadata, other than file modification time (mtime). Thus, unless you have some way to hold on to or re-create that metadata, synchronizing a bucket to a directory in the local file system will not retain the metadata other than mtime. Note that by default, the gsutil rsync command does not copy the ACLs of objects being synchronized and instead will use the default bucket ACL (see "gsutil help defacl"). You can override this behavior with the -p option. See the `Options section `_ to learn how. SLOW CHECKSUMS If you find that CRC32C checksum computation runs slowly, this is likely because you don't have a compiled CRC32c on your system. Try running: gsutil ver -l If the output contains: compiled crcmod: False you are running a Python library for computing CRC32C, which is much slower than using the compiled code. For information on getting a compiled CRC32C implementation, see 'gsutil help crc32c'. LIMITATIONS 1. The gsutil rsync command will only allow non-negative file modification times to be used in its comparisons. This means gsutil rsync will resort to using checksums for any file with a timestamp before 1970-01-01 UTC. 2. The gsutil rsync command considers only the live object version in the source and destination buckets when deciding what to copy / delete. If versioning is enabled in the destination bucket then gsutil rsync's replacing or deleting objects will end up creating versions, but the command doesn't try to make any noncurrent versions match in the source and destination buckets. 3. The gsutil rsync command does not support copying special file types such as sockets, device files, named pipes, or any other non-standard files intended to represent an operating system resource. If you run gsutil rsync on a source directory that includes such files (for example, copying the root directory on Linux that includes /dev ), you should use the -x flag to exclude these files. Otherwise, gsutil rsync may fail or hang. 4. The gsutil rsync command copies changed files in their entirety and does not employ the `rsync delta-transfer algorithm `_ to transfer portions of a changed file. This is because Cloud Storage objects are immutable and no facility exists to read partial object checksums or perform partial replacements. OPTIONS -a canned_acl Sets named canned_acl when uploaded objects created. See "gsutil help acls" for further details. Note that rsync will decide whether or not to perform a copy based only on object size and modification time, not current ACL state. Also see the -p option below. -c Causes the rsync command to compute and compare checksums (instead of comparing mtime) for files if the size of source and destination match. This option increases local disk I/O and run time if either src_url or dst_url are on the local file system. -C If an error occurs, continue to attempt to copy the remaining files. If errors occurred, gsutil's exit status will be non-zero even if this flag is set. This option is implicitly set when running "gsutil -m rsync...". NOTE: -C only applies to the actual copying operation. If an error occurs while iterating over the files in the local directory (e.g., invalid Unicode file name) gsutil will print an error message and abort. -d Delete extra files under dst_url not found under src_url. By default extra files are not deleted. NOTE: this option can delete data quickly if you specify the wrong source/destination combination. See the help section above, "BE CAREFUL WHEN USING -d OPTION!". -e Exclude symlinks. When specified, symbolic links will be ignored. Note that gsutil does not follow directory symlinks, regardless of whether -e is specified. -i This forces rsync to skip any files which exist on the destination and have a modified time that is newer than the source file. (If an existing destination file has a modification time equal to the source file's, it will be updated if the sizes are different.) -j Applies gzip transport encoding to any file upload whose extension matches the -j extension list. This is useful when uploading files with compressible content (such as .js, .css, or .html files) because it saves network bandwidth while also leaving the data uncompressed in Google Cloud Storage. When you specify the -j option, files being uploaded are compressed in-memory and on-the-wire only. Both the local files and Cloud Storage objects remain uncompressed. The uploaded objects retain the Content-Type and name of the original files. Note that if you want to use the top-level -m option to parallelize copies along with the -j/-J options, your performance may be bottlenecked by the "max_upload_compression_buffer_size" boto config option, which is set to 2 GiB by default. This compression buffer size can be changed to a higher limit, e.g.: gsutil -o "GSUtil:max_upload_compression_buffer_size=8G" \ -m rsync -j html,txt /local/source/dir gs://bucket/path -J Applies gzip transport encoding to file uploads. This option works like the -j option described above, but it applies to all uploaded files, regardless of extension. CAUTION: If you use this option and some of the source files don't compress well (e.g., that's often true of binary data), this option may result in longer uploads. -n Causes rsync to run in "dry run" mode, i.e., just outputting what would be copied or deleted without actually doing any copying/deleting. -p Causes ACLs to be preserved when objects are copied. Note that rsync will decide whether or not to perform a copy based only on object size and modification time, not current ACL state. Thus, if the source and destination differ in size or modification time and you run gsutil rsync -p, the file will be copied and ACL preserved. However, if the source and destination don't differ in size or checksum but have different ACLs, running gsutil rsync -p will have no effect. Note that this option has performance and cost implications when using the XML API, as it requires separate HTTP calls for interacting with ACLs. The performance issue can be mitigated to some degree by using gsutil -m rsync to cause parallel synchronization. Also, this option only works if you have OWNER access to all of the objects that are copied. You can avoid the additional performance and cost of using rsync -p if you want all objects in the destination bucket to end up with the same ACL by setting a default object ACL on that bucket instead of using rsync -p. See 'gsutil help defacl'. -P Causes POSIX attributes to be preserved when objects are copied. With this feature enabled, gsutil rsync will copy fields provided by stat. These are the user ID of the owner, the group ID of the owning group, the mode (permissions) of the file, and the access/modification timestamps of the file. For downloads, these attributes will only be set if the source objects were uploaded with this flag enabled. On Windows, this flag will only set and restore access time and modification time. This is because Windows doesn't have a notion of POSIX uid/gid/mode. -R, -r The -R and -r options are synonymous. Causes directories, buckets, and bucket subdirectories to be synchronized recursively. If you neglect to use this option gsutil will make only the top-level directory in the source and destination URLs match, skipping any sub-directories. -u When a file/object is present in both the source and destination, if mtime is available for both, do not perform the copy if the destination mtime is newer. -U Skip objects with unsupported object types instead of failing. Unsupported object types are Amazon S3 Objects in the GLACIER storage class. -x pattern Causes files/objects matching pattern to be excluded, i.e., any matching files/objects are not copied or deleted. Note that the pattern is a `Python regular expression `_, not a wildcard (so, matching any string ending in "abc" would be specified using ".*abc$" rather than "*abc"). Note also that the exclude path is always relative (similar to Unix rsync or tar exclude options). For example, if you run the command: gsutil rsync -x "data.[/\\].*\.txt$" dir gs://my-bucket it skips the file dir/data1/a.txt. You can use regex alternation to specify multiple exclusions, for example: gsutil rsync -x ".*\.txt$|.*\.jpg$" dir gs://my-bucket skips all .txt and .jpg files in dir. NOTE: When using the Windows cmd.exe command line interpreter, use ^ as an escape character instead of \ and escape the | character. When using Windows PowerShell, use ' instead of " and surround the | character with ". -y pattern Similar to the -x option, but the command will first skip directories/prefixes using the provided pattern and then exclude files/objects using the same pattern. This is usually much faster, but won't work as intended with with negative lookahead patterns. For example, if you run the command: gsutil rsync -y "^(?!.*\.txt$).*" dir gs://my-bucket This would first exclude all subdirectories unless they end in .txt before excluding all files except those ending in .txt. Running the same command with the -x option would result in all .txt files being included, regardless of whether they appear in subdirectories.

How about ````json_str.strip().rstrip("`")````?

Currently, the parsing fails on strings with whitespace trailing the backticks, since it is trying to strip backticks from the right side of the string and hits whitespace first.
author: nathan-az

body:
Exactly, my initial intention was to make it able to accept strings which contain zero ending backticks.
The pattern right now describes a group of 1 to 3 backticks and this whole group is not mandatory.

Though I'm still not sure whether that's a good practice. Maybe it's better to demand at least one backtick? 

As a matter of fact, I only added the TWO_BACKTICKS_IN_THE_END & ONE_BACKTICK_IN_THE_END strings on my 2nd commit that contain tests (maybe subliminally I reconsidered my initial choice of making backticks optional).

body: thanks for pr @thaiminhpv! could we add a not explaining the difference between this and FinalStreamingStdOutCallbackHandler? imagine it might otherwise confuse a lot of folks
author: baskaryan
thaiminhpv

body:
@baskaryan 
I think the differences are as follows:

The `FinalStreamingStdOutCallbackHandler`, as the name suggests, it just output the final response of the agent to stdout. User can implement anything such as postprocess or complex logic on top of that.

The `StreamingLastResponseCallbackHandler`, is like the **battery included** version of `FinalStreamingStdOutCallbackHandler`, with additional features that I mentioned earlier in this PR.

In other words:

- If the user needs to custom some complex logic themselves -> go for `FinalStreamingStdOutCallbackHandler`

- If user want to post-process on-the-fly, or use multiple answer-prefix-phrase, or use abnormal detection feature -> go for `FinalStreamingStdOutCallbackHandler`.

I'm not sure where to document the explanation of the difference. Do you have any idea?

author: hwchase17

body:
Both document_id and document can be `Union[str, List[str]]` but we need to do type check. Because embed_documents and update functions takes List for text and document_ids variables. I added a typecheck like that:
```

def update_document(
        self, document_ids: Union[str, List[str]], documents: Union[Document, List[Document]]
    ) -> None:
        """Update a document in the collection.

        Args:
            document_ids (Union[str, List[str]]): IDs of the document to update.
            documents (Union[Document, List[Document]]): Documents to update.
        """
        if isinstance(document_ids, str):
            document_ids = [document_ids]  # Convert to a list if it's a string
        
        if isinstance(documents, Document):
            documents = [documents]  # Convert to a list if it's a Document

        text = [document.page_content for document in documents]
        metadata = [document.metadata for document in documents]
        if self._embedding_function is None:
            raise ValueError(
                "For update, you must specify an embedding function on creation."
            )
        embeddings = self._embedding_function.embed_documents(text)

        self._collection.update(
            ids=document_ids,
            embeddings=embeddings,
            documents=text,
            metadatas=metadata,
        )
```
If you say it's not suitable, a separate method can also be written. But I think #5415 already does that as @Satyam-79 mentioned. However, if you still want me to write it, I can write it and push it to this pull request.

body:
I am using this in a project at the moment and it is working perfectly!
I am using the embedding model text-embedding-ada-002. 

Below is snippet from my codebase. 
Please let me know if this is sufficient. 
```
model_name = "text-embedding-ada-002"

embed = OpenAIEmbeddings(
    model=model_name,
    openai_api_key="..."
)

# switch back to normal index for langchain
index = pinecone.Index("groceries")

vectorstore = Pinecone(
    index, embed.embed_query, text_field
)

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    model_name='gpt-3.5-turbo',
    verbose=True,
    temperature=0.0
)

qa = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(
    llm=llm,
    chain_type="stuff",
    verbose=True,
    chain_type_kwargs={"prompt": PROMPT},
    retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(
    search_type="similarity_score_threshold",
    search_kwargs={"score_threshold": 0.8})
)

```

body:
I don't think so. According to the spec:

> HTML [[RFC 1866](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1866)] defines a powerful means of specifying multimedia
   documents. These multimedia documents consist of a text/html root
   resource (object) and other subsidiary resources (image, video clip,
   applet, etc. objects) referenced by Uniform Resource Identifiers
   (URIs) within the text/html root resource. When an HTML multimedia
   document is retrieved by a browser, each of these component resources
   is individually retrieved in real time from a location, and using a
   protocol, specified by each URI.
   
   https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2557

body:
I like this on a high level! Some things that come to mind as I skim this:

- It's explicitly targeting agents (or rather the agent executor state machine). That's great, I think, especially at first, but I wonder if we want to indicate this in any way via the callback naming since it doesn't support arbitrary "chain" or other workflows.
- I may be misreading, but it doesn't seem like it supports at most one thought at a time, right? I think that's fine to start, just want to understand the limits a bit here.

Re: testing, an integration test would be great. If we could mock a couple of the imports, that would be even better, but I'm not sure we are quite that rigorous on the callback integrations yet.

cc @agola11

body:
If you're using the same regex for many times, then it's better to compile and use it. It should give a performance boost.

```
match_regx = re.compile(f"({minor}){{e<={errs_}}}")
s = match_regx.search(major)
while s is None and errs_ <= errs:
     errs_ += 1
     s = match_regx.search(major)
```

author: dev2049

body:
I would suggest matching the style of MLflow, which uses the variables run_name and experiment_name : 
https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/python_api/mlflow.html#mlflow.run 

However, MLflow [doesn't use the parameter](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/search.html?q=run_tags&check_keywords=yes&area=default) run_tags so I suggest keeping that to 'tags'. The documentation explains that they are tags for the run.

It should be important to note that if we change the parameter names, people who build code with the MLflow callback and set those parameters will have breaking code when they update to a newer version.

body:
I could be missing a potential use case, but right now I can't think of any situations where it needs to be mutable since you'd expect to read the output from the same expected region.
If the concern is to not introduce any unwanted side effects, we could maybe just use kwargs? something like this:

```
    def __init__(self, **kwargs) -> None:
        self.tokens_area = st.empty()
        self.tokens_stream = ""
        self.container = kwargs.get('container', None)
...
    def on_llm_new_token(self, token: str, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        """Run on new LLM token. Only available when streaming is enabled."""
        self.tokens_stream += token
        if self.container:
            with self.container:
                self.tokens_area.write(self.tokens_stream)
        else:
            self.tokens_area.write(self.tokens_stream)
```

body:
@dev2049 @pandemosth 

`verify_ssl` arg has been deprecated, and replaced with:

ssl –
SSL validation mode. None for default SSL check
([ssl.create_default_context()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ssl.html#ssl.create_default_context) is used), False for skip SSL certificate validation, [aiohttp.Fingerprint](https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/client_reference.html#aiohttp.Fingerprint) for fingerprint validation, [ssl.SSLContext](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ssl.html#ssl.SSLContext) for custom SSL certificate validation.
Supersedes verify_ssl, ssl_context and fingerprint parameters.
(From aiohttp docs: https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/client_reference.html#aiohttp.ClientSession.request)

I've updated the code to use `ssl` param

body:
We have a default implementation of `_similarity_search_with_relevance_scores` in the base class that will ensure backwards compatibility while also actually computing the relevance score: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/117e9efa4fac341671c31bab892111011e034437/langchain/vectorstores/base.py#L189-L213 (not just the distance). 

All classes that previously had this ` _similarity_search_with_relevance_scores` implemented in the subclass should have a default relvance score function that will be selected by the select relevance score fun now that will be used, so this should also ensure backwards compatibility

body:
Oh missed  pretty big one -> callback handlers can be called asynchronously, which means one LLM could start before the previous LLM ends, breaking this state tracking as a single variable .

All the `on_llm_?` methods are passed a `run_id: UUID` keyword argument you can use to make sure the state you are tracking is associated with the correct run.

You will probably want to do something like 

```
self.run_map = defaultdict(dict)
```

and then

```
self.run_map[run_id]['on_llm_start_time'] = time()
self.run_map[run_id]['input_texts'] = prompts
...
time_from_start_to_end = self.run_map[run_id]['on_llm_start_time'] -time()
```

adding in the appropriate error handling in case the run id isn't present in the dicitionary.

author: rlancemartin

body:
right. there is some confusion on where it should live. 

the current flow is:
(1) use grobid loader to ingest doc and produce an XML file.
(2) use grobid parser to parse the XML file.

parsers currently operate on blob loaders (inherit from `BaseBlobParser`).

--- 

a few options:

**(1) use a existing blob loader to load PDFs and add the XML generation to the blob parser.**
+ pro: blob loader -> parser flow is a useful design pattern avoiding loader boilderplate duplication
+ con: large code change, UX* less obvious, but does follow pattern from [recent PRs](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6486):

```
loader = GenericLoader.from_filesystem(
    "./example_data/source_code",
    glob="*",
    suffixes=[".pdf"],
    parser= GrobidParser(segment_sentences=True)
)
docs = loader.load()
```

**(2) use existing loader and move parsing logic to text splitter.**
+ pro: intuitive, historical precedent 
+ con: the splitter is specific to this loader (due to ingestion of XML), bloating text_splitter

**(3) move "parser" as a function in the loader.**
+ pro: clear, intuitive UX*, encapsulation of all function in the loader, 
+ con: breaks from blob loader -> parser paradigm, which we may want to move towards

*UX would be same as we currently have: `loader=GrobidLoader(file_path,segment_sentences=False)`

I am not sure whether `BaseTool.description` is always present given the vast library of tools Langchain provided.
However, if the tool is generated from `StructuredTool.from_function(func)` or `Tool.from_function(func)` it will need to provide either a docstring or the argument description.

But `BaseTool.description` could be None although it's a Pydantic field(ie. by subclassing BaseTool with `description: str = None` but I doubt this way the linter will pass.)

Do you think it's better to make this description argument default to an empty string? or possibly set this argument conditionally based on whether the description is present in the tools.

body:
note that `codespell` is not really a full blown spellchecker - it is rather a "finder of common typos", so many misspelled words could exist, and on contrary -- `codespell` could alert about some correct, but rarely used words, which are more typically appearing as typos.  So may be make it explicit here:

```suggestion
Spellchecking for this project is done via [codespell](https://github.com/codespell-project/codespell).
Note that `codespell` finds common typos, so could have false-positive (correctly spelled but rarely used) and false-negatives (not finding misspelled) words.
```

body: Abstraction problem: `MultiQueryRetriever` is designed to handle a variety of LLMChain objects, which in turn can use a variety of `OutputParser` objects. But, `MultiQueryRetriever` class is making assumptions about the structure of the output from the `OutputParser`, which might not always hold true.
author: rlancemartin

body:
One option is passing the `parser_key` as a parameter. Pushes more responsibility onto the user, but also affords the flexibility to supply your own prompt / output parser. Of course, it can be run in "easy mode" with these abstracted away from the user, as shown in the notebook.
```
llm = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0)
retriever_from_llm = MultiQueryRetriever.from_llm(retriever=vectordb.as_retriever(),num_queries=num_queries,llm=llm)
```

body:
## PR Analysis

- 🎯 **Main theme:** The PR is mainly about throwing errors when certain keys are not present in the data.
- 🔍 **Description and title:** No
- 📌 **Type of PR:** Bug fix
- 🐞 **Bug description:** The bug was that the code was not handling cases where certain keys were not present in the data. The fix is to throw an error when these keys are not present.
- 🧪 **Relevant tests added:** No
- ⚠️ **Unrelated changes:** No
- ✨ **Minimal and focused:** Yes, the PR is small and focused on its main theme of throwing errors when certain keys are not present.
## PR Feedback

- 💡 **Suggestions:** The PR lacks a description which is crucial for understanding the context and the reason behind the changes made. Please add a detailed description explaining the changes and their necessity. Also, it would be beneficial to add tests that demonstrate the bug and how the changes fix it.
- 🌱 **Minor suggestions:** Consider using more descriptive error messages. Instead of 'This evaluator requires references, but none were given.', you could specify which keys are missing. This would make debugging easier.
- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**

  - In file `implementations.py` : The code `score = self.choices_map.get(value) if self.choices_map else None` could be improved by handling the case where `value` is not in `self.choices_map`. Instead of returning `None` when `value` is not found, consider throwing an error with a descriptive message. This would make debugging easier and prevent potential issues down the line. [important]
  - In file `implementations.py` : The code `data = {value: outputs[key] for key, value in self.prediction_map.items()}` assumes that all keys in `self.prediction_map` are present in `outputs`. Consider handling the case where a key is not present in `outputs`. [important]

Comment 'CodiumAI please review' to ask for another review after you update the PR.
author: QodoAI-Agent

## PR Analysis

- 🎯 **Main theme:** The PR enhances the delete function in the Pinecone integration by adding the ability to delete vectors by specifying a filter condition or delete all vectors in a namespace.
- 🔍 **Description and title:** Yes
- 📌 **Type of PR:** Enhancement
- 🧪 **Relevant tests added:** No
- ⚠️ **Unrelated changes:** No
- ✨ **Minimal and focused:** Yes, this PR is small and focuses only on enhancing the delete function in the Pinecone integration.
## PR Feedback

- 💡 **Suggestions:** The PR is well-structured and the changes are clear. However, it would be beneficial to include tests that demonstrate the new functionality. This would help ensure that the changes work as expected and prevent potential regressions in the future.
- 🌱 **Minor suggestions:** Consider adding more detailed docstrings for the delete function. This would help users understand the different ways they can use the function and what they should expect as a result.
- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**

  - In the `delete` function, consider handling the case where both `ids` and `filter` are provided. Currently, the function only deletes by `ids` if they are provided, even if a `filter` is also provided. This could lead to unexpected behavior for users. [important]
  - In the `delete` function, consider returning a meaningful message or result when no vectors are deleted because none match the provided `ids` or `filter`. This would provide users with more information about the result of their delete operation. [medium]

Comment 'CodiumAI please review' to ask for another review after you update the PR.
author: QodoAI-Agent

- 🧪 **Relevant tests added:** No

- ✨ **Minimal and focused:** Yes, this PR is small and focuses only on enhancing the delete function in the Pinecone integration.
## PR Feedback

- 💡 **Suggestions:** The PR is well-structured and the code changes are clear. However, it would be beneficial to include tests demonstrating the new functionality. This would ensure that the new features work as expected and help prevent potential regressions in the future.
- 🌱 **Minor suggestions:** Consider adding more detailed docstrings to the delete function. This could include examples of how to use the new filter and delete_all parameters, which would make the function easier to use and understand.
- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**

  - In the delete function in pinecone.py: Consider handling the case where both 'ids' and 'filter' are provided. Currently, if both are provided, only 'ids' will be used and 'filter' will be ignored. This could lead to unexpected behavior for the user. [important]
  - In the delete function in pinecone.py: The function currently returns the result of the delete operation. However, the return type in the function signature is 'None'. Please update the function signature to reflect the actual return type. [important]

body:
seems like `delete` is implemented on base VectorStore class as `delete(self, ids: List[str])`, so we shouldn't change signature (which I recognize Pinecone implementation already did, but should fix that). i suggest we either:
1. update VectorStore.delete to have signature more like `delete(self, ids: Optional[List[str]]=None, **kwargs: Any)` or rename it to `delete_by_ids(self, ids: List[str])` and then we can keep this implementation as is
2. rename this method to something like `delete_with_kwargs` (maybe there's a better name)

think i prefer (1) but curious to hear what others think, cc @rlancemartin @eyurtsev

Thanks for adding. Nit:

Please update your current `load` method to `lazy_load` -

`def lazy_load(self) -> Iterator[Document]:`

And then simply add a new `load` method that calls `lazy_load` - 
```
def load(self) -> List[Document]:
        """Loads a query result from Brave Search engine into a list of Documents."""
        return list(self.lazy_load())
```

body:
I've attempted it, but it seems the results are not quite satisfactory.

 The issue arises because the 'formmatter' module in Langchain currently cannot support missing variables. In other words, if I do not assign a default value, such as an empty string ("") to a missing variable, an error occurs.

The formatting processing mainly takes place in these two functions within the DEFAULT_FORMATTER_MAPPING dictionary:

DEFAULT_FORMATTER_MAPPING: Dict[str, Callable] = {
"f-string": formatter.format,
"jinja2": jinja2_formatter,
}

While the author's suggestions are insightful, they seem to be incompatible with Langchain's current variable handling mechanism. It's quite awkward.

this problem is in issue #7190

body:
Something not part of the PR, but we should do, is default to excluding the `vector` field.

`ElasticVectorSearch#similarity_search_with_score`, doesn't specify that it only cares about `metadata` and `text`. So there is unnecessary work and data transfer returning the vectors and any other fields. 

Similarly, I think `ElasticKnnSearch` should by default return `Document` objects like its parent class when doing KNN search, only returning metadata and text. This way users of the Elastic clients have a nice uniform API. Otherwise, it could be weird to switch from `Document` to `dict` and some returning vectors, others not, etc.
author: benwtrent

body: This should have parity with the parent class. Meaning it should be a `@classmethod` and be used as a ctor for the class, so it should return a `ElasticKnnSearch` not `None`
author: benwtrent

body:
This should effectively override the parent method. So the signature should be:
```
 def add_texts(
        self,
        texts: Iterable[str],
        metadatas: Optional[List[dict]] = None,
        refresh_indices: bool = True,
        ids: Optional[List[str]] = None,
        **kwargs: Any,
    ) -> List[str]:
```

And return generated uuids from indexing if the user didn't provide IDs to index.
author: benwtrent

body:
The metadata is dumped as json now and will be treated as a string when extracted. eg : "metadata": "{"document_id": "xcf15"}"
This field is retrievable but not searchable or filterable. As we may need to pass in a filter like

_filters = {
"document_id eq 'id_01'"
}

retriever = AzureSearchVectorStoreRetriever(
vectorstore = vector_store,
filters=filters,
k = 4)_

extra_fields is an optional dictionary that defaults to 'None'. We will be able to apply filters if a dictionary is passed to add texts function like
extra_fields = [[{'document_id': 'xcf0'},{'document_id': 'xcf1'}],[{'document_id': 'xcf2'},{'document_id': 'xcf3'}]]
vector_store.add_texts(texts, metadatas, extra_fields)

body:
Thanks for your suggestion. I changed it accordingly.

I came across this problem when I wanted to identify the text block using some identifier like _block id_. Initially, my idea was to keep the _extra_ fields as a _list[list[dict]]]_ . 
eg: if there are two blocks of text, the extra fields will be [[{'doc_id':'x'},{block_id':1}],[{'doc_id':'y'},{block_id':2}]]
This will be helpful especially when using question answer with sources, where i can identify the block with a filter

But as you have defined metadatas as a list of dict, I think you have not anticipated this requirement. for me the metadatas was not very useful, as we cannot identify individual blocks with it. Now I have defined extra fields similar to metadatas. But please let me know if you find my previous solution useful. 
If so I should define extra fields as in this commit
#2a0f87e3418997234c99c9f5f5ce077d3e91989f

body:
Weird that we had `_invocation_params` and `_get_invocation_params` that each served different purposes:
1. `_get_invocation_params` was used in callbacks and for cache checking
2. `_invocation_params` used for actually passing params to the openai and azure openai client

Some other options would be:
- Always do `_get_invocation_params` but then either don't call `super()` or change the `self.dict()` call to filter out the `_type` arguments 
- Always do `_get_invocation_params` but then add the api key in a separate method

body:
This returns citations: 
```
from langchain.chains import RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain
qa_chain = RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain.from_chain_type(llm,retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever())
result = qa_chain({"question": question})
```

This returns source documents:
```
from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA
qa_chain = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(llm,retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(),
                                       return_source_documents=True)
result = qa_chain({"query": question})
print(len(result['source_documents']))
result['source_documents'][0]
```

Do those cover the use-case you need here?

loader = GenericLoader.from_filesystem(

)

> I realize this might seem a bit abstract without a practical demonstration, so if you think it would be helpful, I'd be more than happy to whip up a Jupyter notebook.

Hmm, yes please add notebook to clarify.

Pinecone already [accepts metadata filter ](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/bd0c6381f5a6ead453f4c5576f45988a53c28aa5/langchain/vectorstores/pinecone.py#L132)as a dict.

Also, we have [self-query retriever](https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/data_connection/retrievers/how_to/self_query/) that infers metadata from a query.
author: rlancemartin

body:
Hi ! 

Here is a code snippet :

```
from langchain.schema import Document
from langchain.vectorstores import Pinecone
from langchain.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
import pinecone

OPENAI_API_KEY = ""
PINECONE_API_KEY = ""
PINECONE_ENV = ""
PINECONE_INDEX_NAME = ""

pinecone.init(
    api_key=PINECONE_API_KEY,  # find at app.pinecone.io
    environment=PINECONE_ENV,  # next to api key in console
)

index = pinecone.Index(PINECONE_INDEX_NAME)
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(openai_api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY)
vectorstore = Pinecone(index, embeddings.embed_query, "text")

docs = [
    Document(page_content="A bunch of scientists bring back dinosaurs and mayhem breaks loose", metadata={"year": 1993, "rating": 7.7, "genre": ["action", "science fiction"]}),
    Document(page_content="Leo DiCaprio gets lost in a dream within a dream within a dream within a ...", metadata={"year": 2010, "director": "Christopher Nolan", "rating": 8.2}),
    Document(page_content="A psychologist / detective gets lost in a series of dreams within dreams within dreams and Inception reused the idea", metadata={"year": 2006, "director": "Satoshi Kon", "rating": 8.6}),
    Document(page_content="A bunch of normal-sized women are supremely wholesome and some men pine after them", metadata={"year": 2019, "director": "Greta Gerwig", "rating": 8.3}),
    Document(page_content="Toys come alive and have a blast doing so", metadata={"year": 1995, "genre": "animated"}),
    Document(page_content="Three men walk into the Zone, three men walk out of the Zone", metadata={"year": 1979, "rating": 9.9, "director": "Andrei Tarkovsky", "genre": ["science fiction", "thriller"], "rating": 9.9})
]

metadata = [doc.metadata for doc in docs]

vectorstore.search_documents_by_metadata(metadata=metadata)

#Matches all documents that have the same metadata
# Created filter :
# {
#   "$and": [
#     {
#       "year": {
#         "$in": [
#           2019,
#           1993,
#           1995,
#           2006,
#           2010,
#           1979
#         ]
#       }
#     },
#     {
#       "rating": {
#         "$in": [
#           7.7,
#           8.2,
#           8.6,
#           9.9,
#           8.3
#         ]
#       }
#     },
#     {
#       "genre": {
#         "$in": [
#           "thriller",
#           "action",
#           "animated",
#           "science fiction"
#         ]
#       }
#     },
#     {
#       "director": {
#         "$in": [
#           "Satoshi Kon",
#           "Andrei Tarkovsky",
#           "Greta Gerwig",
#           "Christopher Nolan"
#         ]
#       }
#     }
#   ]
# }


#You can also search for a single field of metadata
metadata = [{
    "director" : [doc.metadata['director'] for doc in docs if 'director' in doc.metadata]
}]

vectorstore.search_documents_by_metadata(metadata=metadata)

# Created filter : 
# {
#   "$and": [
#     {
#       "director": {
#         "$in": [
#           "Satoshi Kon",
#           "Christopher Nolan",
#           "Greta Gerwig",
#           "Andrei Tarkovsky"
#         ]
#       }
#     }
#   ]
# }
```


So, while it's true that you can already use filters with the current setup, this function creates the filters for you on the fly based on the metadata directly. Now, I know it doesn't leave much room for customization, but when it comes to doing a quick sweep based on metadata, it gets the job done efficiently.

Compared to the two other alternatives, this function does not always require a text input. It automatically generates an empty vector, eliminating the need to create an embedding. This makes it a little bit more time and money efficient.

Just a quick note – this approach tends to work best when all the metadata share the same keys. I realize it might not be as versatile as we'd like, but it's pretty efficient within its niche. I'm open to your thoughts though – if you feel this isn't sufficiently generalized, please do let me know!

body: As a small tip, you can use `exclude` instead of `disable` if you don't ever plan on using the `ner` component. By excluding it, it isn't even loaded and won't be serialized. If you only disable it, it'll be loaded and serialized, though not run until you enable it again.

body:
Extracting embedding for words takes too much vectors and query checker will not be able to check that long sql. Putting the parser here will make the chain have direct control to the parser.

Also, for better readability we think dumping direct result from LLM is better than the parsed one.

For example 
```sql
SELECT * FROM table ORDER BY DISTANCE(vector, NeuralArray(langchain)) LIMIT 5
```
Or you have to dealing with
```sql
SELECT * FROM table ORDER BY distance(vector, [0.2314, 0.132415, ..., 0.13941] LIMIT 5
```
which does not make any sense to human.

body:
Yes this is an issue we found when playing with self-query. We have opened a PR #6737 to fix this.

The major issue is that the CONTAIN comparator sometimes takes place on non-array columns and LIKE comparator sometimes appears on non-string columns. I do agree that this may change the behavior. but since these two comparators were new and added in #6143, I do think this fix is necessary which should improve the prompt stability and all new features built on this may be enhanced.

Or we can leave this unchanged and open another PR for dynamic prompt for comparator?

body:
We need to avoid having phoenix as a dependency here.

1. Users of Arize are not Phoenix users and vice-versa
2. The Arize client has the right abstractions for logging inferences - phoenix is a server runtime

It's important that these callback managers are fine-grained to specific use-cases so that they can be composed together. For that reason I wouldn't couple phoenix.

currently someone could already pass in buffer as part of **kwargs and that'd be passed in at instantiation, but then overwritten by either existing_summary or messages, which is kinda confusing.

what if we did
```python
  @classmethod
  def from_messages(
      cls,
      llm: BaseLanguageModel,
       chat_memory: BaseChatMessageHistory,
       *,
       summarize_step: int = 2,
       buffer: str = "",
       **kwargs:  Any
   ) -> ConversationSummaryMemory:
     obj = cls(llm=llm, chat_memory=chat_memory, buffer=buffer, **kwargs)
     if not buffer:
       for i in range(0, len()...)):
         obj.buffer = obj.predict_new_summary(...)
     return obj

body:
The example in the notebook appears to be wrapping Chroma w/ `add_texts` / `get_relevant_documents` functionality. 
But it doesn't demonstrate added / novel functionality vs what you get from working with Chroma directly. Your package does [much more](https://github.com/csunny/DB-GPT), esp text-to-SQL.

Text-to-SQL may be the more novel feature, as shown in your [docs](https://db-gpt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/use_cases/sql_generation_and_diagnosis.html), to highlight in the notebook? And, with that in mind, perhaps a more narrow and interesting use-case would be a text-to-SQL retriever where it serves as a bridge between nature language and SQL DB.  We do have [SQLDatabaseChain](https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/chains/popular/sqlite), but this is an area of high interest and alternative retrieval methods for SQL are interesting.
author: rlancemartin

as noted below, based on looking at [your package](https://github.com/csunny/DB-GPT), some of the primary features is SQL language capabilities. Focusing on this w/ a new text-to-SQL retriever would be interesting since this is an area of high interest. We do have some existing solutions here ([SQLDatabaseChain](https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/chains/popular/sqlite)) but more option in this area seem important to have.

body:
this introduces the converse problem, of making it hard to create a map reduce docs chain with *only* callbacks on the MapReduceDocumentsChain.

maybe we call this arg something like `shared_callbacks`? and for the final map reduce chain we check if kwargs contains 'callbacks', and only used shared_callbacks if it doesn't
author: baskaryan

body:
is that kind of flexibility thing that people want? I think this is an area where I'm a little confused on best practices in langchain. 

What I feel like I'm seeing in *most* of the codebase is that callbacks are passed into agent/chain/llm constructors or utility functions, and that means "all children of this object get the same callbacks" are passed down to child chains / llms / etc.
author: alecf

body:
It might be worth noting that the docs call out this general pattern here:

> The `callbacks` argument is available on most objects throughout the API (Chains, Models, Tools, Agents, etc.) in two different places:
>
> - **Constructor callbacks**: defined in the constructor, eg. `LLMChain(callbacks=[handler], tags=['a-tag'])`, which will be used for all calls made on that object, and will be scoped to that object only, eg. if you pass a handler to the `LLMChain` constructor, it will not be used by the Model attached to that chain.
> - **Request callbacks**: defined in the `run()`/`apply()` methods used for issuing a request, eg. `chain.run(input, callbacks=[handler])`, which will be used for that specific request only, and all sub-requests that it contains (eg. a call to an LLMChain triggers a call to a Model, which uses the same handler passed in the `call()` method).
> 
> The `verbose` argument is available on most objects throughout the API (Chains, Models, Tools, Agents, etc.) as a constructor argument, eg. `LLMChain(verbose=True)`, and it is equivalent to passing a `ConsoleCallbackHandler` to the `callbacks` argument of that object and all child objects. This is useful for debugging, as it will log all events to the console.

i.e. the notion of "shared" callbacks is the default rather than the exception.

This particular root function (`load_summarize_chain`) is technically not a constructor but it is doing a tremendous amount of scaffolding and calling constructors that would otherwise take `callbacks`. 

The way this case seems to be addressed elsewhere in the codebase is to add a `xxx_kwargs` parameter, e.g. `agent_kwargs`. So here you could have `doc_chain_kwargs` but personally that feels like an awkward way to change this interface to support what would seem to me to be a somewhat esoteric case (supporting *only* callbacks on MapReduceDocumentsChain)
author: alecf
